## Thread: OT : Salaries & Contractors — 17 this month / 35 total

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Salaries & Contractors

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <leahydon@nospamplease.netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T15:06:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TQh3e.23653$w63.1365913@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<O32%d.727582$Xk.88626@pd7tw3no> <mbg%d.12192$DW.2090@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```

> Choosing a career as a contractor is an individual choice. Choosing to use 
> a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It's not always a free choice in my neck of the woods (greater Toronto 
area).  Some clients now *require* contractors to work through an agency 
that is on their 'preferred suppliers list'.  The 'preferred suppliers' have 
pre-negotiated fixed rates with the client, which gives the client some cost 
certainty.  This applies even if the contractor is incorporated.

Aside from cost certainty, there are two other justifications cited for this 
rule:

1.  By interposing a third party between the client and the contractor, it 
reinforces the fact that the contractor is *not* an employee of the client.
2.  All of the client's financial dealings are with the third party, which 
is a 'trusted supplier'.  Trusted, that is, to collect the GST (Goods and 
Services Tax) required by Canadian law *and* to remit it to the Government.

Whatever the reasons, one thing that I have learned after 15 years 
contracting is that you have to play the game by their rules if you want to 
stay in the consulting business.

Sometimes the system works to your advantage.  I found the job I wanted, and 
then asked the client for a list of  their 'preferred vendors'.  I then 
worked my way through the list until I found the agency that could offer me 
the best deal.

'Best deal' for me, is a combination of rate and *service*.  I turned down 
one agency that offered a slightly higher rate because they couldn't 
guarantee that I would be paid on time.  "You get paid when we get paid" was 
their motto.  Their contractors are paid several weeks after the invoice 
date and they never know if the cheque is going to bounce or not.
```

```
[stunned silence]

shouldn't be working there.'
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Salaries & Contractors

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T02:11:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a523e.861609$Xk.202669@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6JJ2e.19820$eQ6.4473@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<O32%d.727582$Xk.88626@pd7tw3no> <6JJ2e.19820$eQ6.4473@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Eileen wrote:
> I don't have a clue what the exchange rate is now but I've seen similar jobs
> in the paper here for 1/2 that salary in US dollars.

Eileen,

On the exchange rate - just bought Winzip, so shows up on statement as 
$US 29.00 = $CDN 35.86. Guess that has to be a current exchange rate of 
around 1.23.

So my previous Calgary salary range, $64,609 to $78,604 pa. comes out at 
$US 52,527 - 63,905 pa. So if we take half of the Canadian figure, (your 
basis for comparison), still suggest your job postings are still low at 
$US 32,304 - 39,302.

Having stretched my well known mathematical abilities to do the above, 
*my* Eileen said, "Why don't you look at the Business Section in the 
Calgary Herald ?". There you go - $CDN 1.00 = US 82.17 cents.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Salaries & Contractors

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T14:50:45+00:00

- **References:** `<O32%d.727582$Xk.88626@pd7tw3no> <6JJ2e.19820$eQ6.4473@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com>`

```
What I would need as a contractor in NYC or in Alabama would not be similar at
all.    It's not how much I earn that I care about - it's how much I take home
after the expenses of living there.


I never understood the demand about pro athletes who work for Canadian teams
that they get paid in U.S. Dollars.   The exchange rate doesn't fluctuate that
much.    Just negotiate your salary for what you're satisfied with with either
currency.   It isn't the currency that matters, it's the money.
```

```
are lower!'?'

(http://www.homefair.com/homefair/calc/salcalc.html is - for residents of

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Salaries & Contractors

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Message-ID:** `<d2jqo1$ev6$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<O32%d.727582$Xk.88626@pd7tw3no> <d2jn45$2ea$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d2jppn$mlq$1@panix5.panix.com> <hZd3e.16812$DW.10872@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <hZd3e.16812$DW.10872@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d2jppn$mlq$1@panix5.panix.com...
>> In article <d2jn45$2ea$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Doc, I'm convinced your personal hero is Vince Lombardi.

Your conviction is not supported by my own impression of myself... but 
what do I know about me, anyhow?

>
>Unfortunately, I think your hero workship is misplaced, based on bad input.
>
>Mr. Lombardi stated  "Winning is the Only Thing", not "Money is the Only
>Thing."

I don't believe that money is the only thing... but it is a part of Doing 
Business which I believe needs to be considered.  For example... I'm more 
than happy to choose a contract that provides more interesting work at a 
lower rate because I find it to be more interesting, I am *not* happy to 
be told 'Well, since the work is interesting you will have to accept a 
lower rate for it.'

While work has value to me in both financial (what I can get paid for it) 
and intangible ('interesting') terms the work's value to a client is - 
based on my experience - most often determined by ROI or the potential for 
increased income.  Since our sets of consideration most readily intersect 
in the financial area I try to phrase my arguments in the most common 
language... while they might not consider what I find interesting (as 
several thread here about language might demonstrate) to have merit they 
seem to be able to converse about making a buck fairly well.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Salaries & Contractors

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net>
- **Date:** 2005-04-02T04:13:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3p2s419fhbsd18m3sij8563sqv3jtjs4vt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<O32%d.727582$Xk.88626@pd7tw3no> <6JJ2e.19820$eQ6.4473@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com> <d2jn45$2ea$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d2jppn$mlq$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 1 Apr 2005 10:36:23 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <d2jn45$2ea$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
>Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>are lower!'?'

Generally, contractors do better in high cost of living places. Cost
of living indexes reflect primarily cost of home ownership. Apartments
cost about the same everywhere -- $600-900/mo. Exceptions are
Manhattan, San Francisco and downtown Chicago. Even in those places,
if you're willing to commute via train or bus, you can find an
apartment costing $900/mo or less. My $900/mo apartment in Parsippany,
NJ, was 45 minutes from Wall Street and Midtown Manhattan by bus. I
wouldn't do the commute, I want to go home for lunch, but many
contractors do.

High cost of living places are better because:

1) Higher per diem means substantial tax savings. Middle America at
$100/day vs. NYC at $200/day means additional  $26K/yr in tax exempt
income. At 28%, that means you pay $4K less. If the apartment costs
$300/mo more, you're still even or ahead. 

2)It's more likely you can find another high-paying contract in the
same city, thus avoiding moving expense. 

3)More entertainment options. Boredom will drive you crazy in
Bentonville and Cedar Rapids, unless you're really creative. The best
places aren't necessarily the biggest cities. The best place I lived
was Jacksonville, Fl. The city sucked but it was close to the natural
beauty at Okefenokee, Merritt Island and Fernandina Beach. While
living near NYC, I attended concerts at Madison Square Garden, pizza
places in Brooklyn, Soho, Central Park, etc. While living near
Chicago, I visited Navy Pier, great parks, Polish neighborhoods, etc. 

>(http://www.homefair.com/homefair/calc/salcalc.html is - for residents of 
>the USA or folks considering working there - a useful tool)

It's not useful unless you own a home in both the old and new place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Salaries & Contractors

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2005-04-02T14:01:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2m8kl$jtm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<O32%d.727582$Xk.88626@pd7tw3no> <6JJ2e.19820$eQ6.4473@newssvr31.news.prodigy.com> <d2jn45$2ea$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d2jppn$mlq$1@panix5.panix.com> <3p2s419fhbsd18m3sij8563sqv3jtjs4vt@4ax.com>`

```

On  1-Apr-2005, Robert Wagner <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:

> My $900/mo apartment in Parsippany,
> NJ, was 45 minutes from Wall Street and Midtown Manhattan by bus. I
> wouldn't do the commute, I want to go home for lunch, but many
> contractors do.

I like programming.    My cost of having a job isn't the work I do - it's that
time spent doing that work is time not spent doing things I like better.

My company might get 8 hours of work from me today.    But if I live an hour
away, I am paying 10 hours of my time.    They have to be satisfied that they
are getting value for the 8 hours that they buying - and I have to be satisfied
that I'm getting value for the 10 hours I'm spending.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a36u41poqjj49bt1aemkf36c4bva0qmq1s@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<O32%d.727582$Xk.88626@pd7tw3no> <d2jn45$2ea$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <d2jppn$mlq$1@panix5.panix.com> <3p2s419fhbsd18m3sij8563sqv3jtjs4vt@4ax.com> <d2makb$26c$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 2 Apr 2005 09:35:55 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3p2s419fhbsd18m3sij8563sqv3jtjs4vt@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <spamblocker-robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>find unsatisfactory?

I entered two medium income cities where I lived recently:
                 
                                   relative to 100K in NJ
Parsippany, NJ    Rent:  80,789
Edina, MN           Own:   80,789

No way. Things cost more in MN. Two eggs, hash browns, toast and
coffee
In NJ:  2.99
In MN: 7.50

Why are numbers the same? Lets try the next towns over. Montclair NJ
is an Old Money place where the least expensive house costs over 500K.
If you saw the movie Cheaper By The Dozen, that's Montclair.
Bloomington MN is an upper middle-class  suburb of Minneapolis where
houses cost 300-400K. 

Montclair, NJ         Rent: 112,257
Bloomington, MN  Own:  77,054 

Renter doesn't look right. Let's try the big city.

Montclair, NJ      Rent:  115,661
Minneapolis, MN  Own:   82,995

Maybe there's an error in Montclair data. Let's try an equally
expensive upper-crust NJ place:

Morristown, NJ     Rent: 91,463
Minneapolis, MN  Own:  89,859

They think Montclair is 20% cheaper than Morristown? No way; it's
higher. Let's look at the database:

                                            	Montclair Morristown
Minneapolis	Bloomington
Median Household Income  	$74,894   57,562  	37,974
54,628 
Cost of Living Index (U.S. = 100)  147.43  136.18  	122.36
113.60 
3-Bedroom House Price 		 383,000  328,290  	266,650
235,000 	
2-Bedroom Apartment Rent            850      1,300  		1,050
1,000 

Ah, the apartment rents are estimates. They are comparing apples with
oranges. An  $850 apartment in Montclair is a 50 year-old walk-up over
a storefront. A $1,000 apartment in Bloomington is in a new luxury
complex with tennis courts, whirlpool, landscaped grounds, heated
underground parking and has a nature preserve across the street. A
similar apartment in Edina or Eden Prairie goes for $850 (I live in
one).

A $1,300 apartment in Morristown is in a 'regular' apartment building.
It costs so much because it's in Morristown. A few miles away in
Parsippany, a similar apartment costs $900.

Montclair and Morristown have new luxury apartments comparable to the
one in Bloomington. They rent for $1,600 and up. 

Housing price and Income look about right, for 10 years ago. Add 50%
for today's figures, 100% for NJ houses.

 A renter moving from Montclair to Minneapolis needs $15K more because
.. rent is 1.8K higher. What's the other 13.2K for?  A renter moving
from Morristown to Minneapolis needs 10K less because .. rent is 3K
lower?  They're using incorrect apartment rates as the base for
computing their own cost of living index. They're getting the wrong
answers.

Bottom line: manufactured goods (stuff sold at Wal-Mart + automobiles)
costs the same everywhere in the US. Food and energy vary place to
place for reasons that defy logic, but variations cancel each other,
so the difference is not significant. Services (restaurants, haircuts,
taxis) reflect what the market will bear, so are higher in affluent
places. One can rent an above average to luxury apartment anywhere for
less than $1,000. If the rate is too high in one town or neighborhood,
go to the next, and the next. You'll soon find one that was overbuilt
and is hungry for tenants. 

Do not use internet Apartment Finder sites. Apartments there tend to
be overpriced and overhyped. I go to Yahoo Yellow Pages, put in the
office address and get a list of ALL apartments sorted by distance.
After I've been there a week, in a hotel, I drive around and visit
them, with preference to well-kept cars in the parking lot and lots of
vacancies. An in-person visit is the only way you can decide; you
can't tell from Web pages or phone calls. 

Do ask for month-to-month rather than a fixed-term lease, and be
prepared to pay 10% more for it. If they won't consider it, move on.

In East Coast cities, especially New York, many apartments are not in
professionally managed apartment buildings but rather in private homes
or 'two family homes', an euphemism for apartments disguised to look
like a house. They're owned by amateur investors and the only way to
find them is through ads in throwaway newspapers like Penny Saver.
Owners will drive you crazy with ideosyncratic rules and unethically
keep your security deposit for some flimsy or invented 'damage' you
allegedly caused.  Also, you usually have to pay a real estate agent
one month's rent for 'finder's fee'. I played in that market once, on
Long Island. Never again. The apartment was wonderful, the ground
floor of a 'house' half a mile from the office. But the $1,600 rent
was too high and the unbusinesslike relationship, ignoble. My advice
is to avoid this secondary market and stick with professionally
managed 'regular' apartments. As a last resort, if you have no choice,
post your deposit in escrow with a third-party who will inspect the
place before and after, and write that into the lease. Don't expect
honesty or fairness from an amateur landlord, no matter friendly he or
she is at first meeting.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-02T00:49:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b4ubnF6g5n3rU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9Tpp1rGuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <d2erlm$ec3$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d2erlm$ec3$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <9Tpp1rGuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>,
> Lueko Willms <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Doc, I would be very wary about releasing a procedure that relies on window
co-ordinates and keystrokes.

The equivalent to JCL on a mainframe can be achieved by Windows Host
Scripting on a Windows box.

It costs nothing, is already installed with your Operating System, and
utilises a "proper" language that is very easy to pick up.

I have found it to be extremely useful for application installs where I want
things configured, files created, other files accessed conditions tested and
so on.

For incremental backups you could easily run the programs you do now, under
control of a windows script; you can even automatically schedule it using
the Windows scheduler so your non-diligent user doesn't have to do ANYTHING.
(He would get a message box telling him to load an appropriately labelled CD
in the drive and optionally informing him that backups were about to be run
and was that OK, or would he like to defer it..or whatever you design into
the process. (You have complete controlover the process and can make it do
whatever you want to with whatever mix of manual/automatic you decide is
appropriate. Your backup procedures could be activated by clicking an icon
on the desktop, for example.)

From Windows Host Scripting you can run any program you want to, pass
whatever parameters it requires, access the file system and check whether
files exist, create, update, and delete them, as well as checking what
happened and interfacing to the user via message boxes (you can also get
input if you want to).

It really has all the facilities we used to enjoy with DOS procedures, and
then some. JCL is primitive by comparison.

I turned to it in desperation for a JCL equivalent, about 3 years ago and
have never looked back.

If you decide to try this route and need any help, mail me privately.

Keystroke macros are really NOT the answer here...

Pete.

PS check out: http://www.winguides.com/scripting/
```

```
Hi Leuko,

to answer your question:

I use mainly JavaScript. It is pretty simple to use and, although it has no
affiliation with Java, the syntax and concepts are similar.

I do use VBScript occasionally, mainly because I find it a bit tiresome to
keep 'translating' code samples from VBScript into JScript.

It really doesn't matter for WSH, as either or both can be freely
interchanged just as they can on the web.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-05T01:26:14+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`

```

"Lueko Willms" <l.willms@jpberlin.de> wrote in message
news:9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de...
> .    On  02.04.05
>   wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes. But I originally built them for ASP and then 'converted' them. They are
all COM compliant.

(I have been captivated by this technology for the last 6 years now and I
think it is amazing.)

> PD> It really doesn't matter for WSH, as either or both can be freely
> PD> interchanged just as they can on the web.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's a bit like shooting yourself in the foot.

I don't share the paranoia that is often displayed by some about the
possibilityof viruses being spread by VBScript. They can be spread just as
easily by Java, JavaScript, JScript or  VBA macros in MS applications, or
any other web scripting. Why pick on VBScript? None of the scripting
languages can do anything that the user is not permitted to do. If proper
permissions are in place and the Browser has been updated, security is no
worse than under any other scenario.

If you keep inhibiting functionality because it MIGHT be dangerous, there is
little point in then complaining that the software is not feature rich.

VBScript serves a useful purpose, as does JavaScript.  Some people run their
Browsers with cookies inhibited, thinking it makes them safe (it
doesn''t...http headers can contain cookies even if they are not written to
your machine). The fact is that Perl, PHP, and probably (I don't know
because I have never used it) Python, can all be used to host or even create
malware.

As soon as you connect your machine to the internet, you are at risk. And if
you think using non-MS software is a passport to smugness, you are sadly
mistaken.

(Denigrate MS software on its merits (or lack of them) but don't pretend the
alternatives are watertight; they are not.)

And what exactly is the risk? I have never had my machine off the air for
more than 2 hours because of a virus, and that was in the days when there
was no anti-virus protection. I run a complete virus check every Sunday (as
well as on-demand, of course...). Usually Panda detects a couple of viruses
that have never been activated and removes them, Registry First Aid and
Hijack This clean up the Trojans, AdAware and XoftSpy remove the data miners
and tracking cookies, and we start Monday with a clean slate and an image
backup... How big is the risk? For me, it isn't worth disabling features in
my software. However, I accept that other people may be more risk averse
than I, and I don't think they are wrong for being so.

It is a personal choice and mine is...

Machine gets infected. So what? It is an inconvenience.

Anyway, getting back to the point... WSH is capable of interchanging
VBScript and JScript, and it DOESN'T use Internet Explorer to run either of
them.

<snip>

> Es gibt manche Leute die nicht eher h�ren bis man ihnen die Ohren
abschneidet. -G.C.Lichtenberg
>
"There are many people who never hear of honour until someone cuts their
ears off "(?) Sorry, I don't have a German dictionary to hand... what am I
missing here? Bewildered by this :-)

Pete.
```

```
>> abschneidet. -G.C.Lichtenberg

PD> "There are many people who never hear of honour until someone cuts
PD> their ears off "(?) Sorry, I don't have a German dictionary to
PD> hand... what am I missing here? Bewildered by this :-)

   No, the translation would be:

   "There are people who don't listen until their ears are cut off"

or alternatively:

   "There are people who don't [want to] hear until their ears are cut  
off"


   You probably misunderstood "eher" and confused it with "Ehre".  
"Ehre" means honor, "eher" means 1. earlier, sooner, before; 2. (in  
the form used above, normally as "nicht eher als") not until; 3.  
rather, sooner, preferably; and some other meanings, like 'rather',  
'more'.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.mlwerke.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

"Die Interessen der Nation lassen sich nicht anders formulieren als unter
dem Gesichtspunkt der herrschenden Klasse oder der Klasse, die die
Herrschaft anstrebt."            - Leo Trotzki         (27. Januar 1932)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-04T13:04:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net>`

```
> They can be spread just as easily by Java, JavaScript, JScript or
VBA macros in MS
> applications,

I think that you meant to say J++ when you had 'Java'.

> or any other web scripting.

No. The point is that malware is often using Windows _features_.  For
example http://www.mikx.de/scrollbar/

> The fact is that Perl, PHP, and probably (I don't know
> because I have never used it) Python, can all be used to host or even
create
> malware.

No. These are server side scripting languages while Javascript and
VBScript are client side.  There is a vast difference.  That is, when
you connect your client machine to a potentially malicious server the
Perl, PHP, Python scripts run on the remote server and send you HTML,
the Javascript and VBScript runs on _your_ machine.

Now JS and VBS are running in a 'sandbox' inside your browser so they
can only do what that allows them to.  With MS IE it is allowed to do
more, by design, or can access other features (such as drag and drop).

> And what exactly is the risk?

Viruses used to just do nasty things, like delete files, but now they
are much more useful.  For example a conservitive estimate is one
million Windows zombie spam mailers.  Machines that quietly wait for
instructions from their 'owner' to send out spam mail.  The user
probably never notices the extra traffic.

I wouldn't run any machine, especially not a Windows machine, unless it
was behind a genuine firewall.  I use old 486s, a 386 with a 1.44
floppy drive will do, and a couple of network cards to run Freesco or
Smoothwall,  there are many similar free products.  This runs headless
(no monitor) and acts as a firewall, router, gateway, and can do other
server functions. I put up a new one a couple of weeks ago and watched
the logs as it went on line.  Within a minute it had logged probes on
various ports vulnerable to Windows as well as probes on telnet, ftp
and ssh ports.

> Usually Panda detects a couple of viruses that have never been
activated and removes them,

If you are getting _anything_ then you are being too complacent.  The
latest technology is defeating virus checkers by infecting the
underlying Windows file system levels so that the scan gets a false
report of file sizes and dates, as it would be if the virus wasn't
there.

> Machine gets infected. So what? It is an inconvenience.

It is also an inconvenience to all the mailusers that you may be
pumping spam out to.  For those with dial-up connection it is more than
an inconvenience when they silently dial 0900 numbers in Nigeria.

There was a case just a month or so ago where Xtra agreed to write off
a couple of thousand dollars in excess bandwidth charges where a
Windows machines sent out Gigabytes of spam.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net> <1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com...
> > They can be spread just as easily by Java, JavaScript, JScript or
> VBA macros in MS
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, Java scriptlets and servlets can also carry malware. The fact they they
are 'fenced' or 'sandboxed' as you called it does not prevent them
activating other servers by URL.

It does not require foundation classes.

> > or any other web scripting.
>
> No. The point is that malware is often using Windows _features_.  For
> example http://www.mikx.de/scrollbar/
>
This is really a pretty old chestnut, Richard and has been plugged for
nearly 6 months now... I tried the test on his page and my Browser (IE6,
with updates applied) was not vulnerable.


> > The fact is that Perl, PHP, and probably (I don't know
> > because I have never used it) Python, can all be used to host or even
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> VBScript are client side.  There is a vast difference.

I totally understand the difference between client and server side (and you
have mentioned before that JavaScript is client side, when it can be used
for both [RUNAT=SERVER]).  A client side script in ANY language that
supports access to COM components on the client, CAN be used for malware. On
Non-windows machines the same effect can be obtained with Java beans on the
client or using CORBA on a client.


> That is, when
> you connect your client machine to a potentially malicious server the
> Perl, PHP, Python scripts run on the remote server and send you HTML,
> the Javascript and VBScript runs on _your_ machine.
>
If the server is malicious it can send you a lot more than HTML. Server
push, HTML containing client pull and SSI, are all potentially harmful, yet
I use them all the time, without problem.

> Now JS and VBS are running in a 'sandbox' inside your browser so they
> can only do what that allows them to.  With MS IE it is allowed to do
> more, by design, or can access other features (such as drag and drop).
>
Yes, that is fair comment. However it is also true that many of these
'holes' have been plugged in later versions of IE.

> > And what exactly is the risk?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> probably never notices the extra traffic.

I run an STMP mail server under IIS. It has been attacked once and someone
in Taiwan tried to hijack it for exactly the purposes you describe. The
checks and balances I had in place (which I am certainly not going to
describe here) alerted me before it had sent a dozen mails. It took 15
minutes to adjust the firewall and some SMTP options. Since then I have
never had a problem.

>
> I wouldn't run any machine, especially not a Windows machine, unless it
> was behind a genuine firewall.

Yes, on that we can certainly agree.


> I use old 486s, a 386 with a 1.44
> floppy drive will do, and a couple of network cards to run Freesco or
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, I also note probes every day, but they don't succeed; that is why we
have firewalls.



> > Usually Panda detects a couple of viruses that have never been
> activated and removes them,
>
> If you are getting _anything_ then you are being too complacent.
(in your opinion. I don't see it so...) The worst that could happen is I
lose a day. The system is restored as it was yesterday before the disaster
(whether it be malware or hardware failure) happened. I can tolerate that.
I'd rather have the facilities and use them, knowing and having weighed the
risks, than simply disable them because there MIGHT be a problem. But, as I
have tried to stress all along, it is a very personal decision and other
people won't see it that way. I am not advocating that others should do what
I do; simply that it works for me.

> The
> latest technology is defeating virus checkers by infecting the
> underlying Windows file system levels so that the scan gets a false
> report of file sizes and dates, as it would be if the virus wasn't
> there.

The 'underlying Windows file system levels' are NTFS on my system. It is as
secure as anything can be in this environment. I have been doing a fair bit
of work with the file system, and the WSH paper I have recently released
here deals with using these objects. I agree with you that this COULD be
subverted by script code (the facilities to change file size, dates, etc.
are certainly there within the object methods) but on a properly configured
system such a script would never get to run UNLESS the user allowed it to,
and had permissions to the objects it was manipulating.  (As most of them
are System Owned it is not that easy... you would need Admin priveleges for
a start...It could not be run from within the latest editions of  IE and
SP2.). But, obviously, it COULD happen, if a user sat down and knowingly ran
a script that did it. This comes back to the point I made in my earlier
post: ANY programmable system can have malware on it if people decide to sit
down and write it, and the language being used allows it.(And sometimes
legitimate requirements that the language MUST allow, can be subverted to
bad purposes.)

What does sometimes happen on my system is that some undetected viruses get
into the notorious 'Local Storage' area and are archived by windows into
'secret storage'. Panda finds them when it does a full scan and would find
them if any of these files were attempted to be activated. I clear 'secret
storage' manually (I don't use erasure tools because I don't feel confident
about what they do) about every three months. It pisses Windows off, and it
sulks a bit before getting back to normal after an hour or so.

>
> > Machine gets infected. So what? It is an inconvenience.
>
> It is also an inconvenience to all the mailusers that you may be
> pumping spam out to.

But I'm not, so that is just speculation on your part.

> For those with dial-up connection it is more than
> an inconvenience when they silently dial 0900 numbers in Nigeria.
>

And that is just using an extreme case that has no relevance on my personal
machine, to reinforce your point (which I don't entirely disagree with
anyway). Any good virus checker should notify the minute you download a
dialer (and Panda certainly does.) Other checks I have in place watch
anything that tries to dial anywhere, and that is over and above the
firewall.

I am not dismissing your concern, just balancing it. (And reminding you that
my comments only apply to MY system).

> There was a case just a month or so ago where Xtra agreed to write off
> a couple of thousand dollars in excess bandwidth charges where a
> Windows machines sent out Gigabytes of spam.
>
I don't use Xtra and wouldn't. (So it wasn't my machine... :-))

Richard I have nothing but respect for what you do and how you do it. But
you have to accept that there is a diversity of approaches in IT.

My comments at the time I posted them, were confined entirely to my personal
machine and experience. Yet when I posted I had a bet with myself there
would be comments from you and Leuko... How could I know that?  Because I
know that you both are anti Windows and anti MS. And I totally respect your
right to be so. I don't even think you are wrong, but I do think the
reaction is more emotional than logical.

For millions of people around the world, Windows HAS to be a reasonable
platform. I use it because, in the days when I was trying to make money
writing software, that was what my customers used and, as you know, I
totally believe in markets.

I am not blind to its shortcomings, but neither am I blind to the fact that
there is NO perfect approach. And I refuse to get emotional about computer
software. If it works; good. If it doesn't; fix it or change it. For me (and
I am not the only one...) Windows works. (OK, it needs a lot of fixing, but
MS are doing that...).

Personally, I find XP Pro to be an excellent OS. I find IE6 meets my browser
needs adequately. But that doesn't mean I won't use Linux or Firefox. I know
they are also good products. I changed from MS Access to MySQL and have
never regretted it, but I had an incentive to do so. I can't see me
investing more time in the learning curve for new software when what I have
serves me fine.(I have already invested huge amounts of time learning to get
the best out of what I have, I would need a VERY good reason to start
over...)

However, that is one man's opinion and I respect the right of others to
disagree.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-04T20:05:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112670347.953014.47280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3be99sF6hhl2tU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net> <1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <3be99sF6hhl2tU1@individual.net>`

```
> No, Java scriptlets and servlets can also carry malware.

Servlets run the server (hence the name) and cannot 'carry' anything to
your machine.

By 'Java "scriplets"' I presiume that you mean applets, which do run on
the client machine.

> The fact they they are 'fenced' or 'sandboxed' as you called it does
not prevent them
> activating other servers by URL.

So can HTML simply by having an IMG tag.  Stuff running on a server in
some other country is not executing code on your machine.

> This is really a pretty old chestnut, Richard and has been plugged
for
> nearly 6 months now...

The point is that it was not a 'bug' but a 'feature' that was being
exploited, and tens or hundreds of millions of machines are still
vulnerable.

> No. These are server side scripting languages while Javascript and
> VBScript are client side.  There is a vast difference.

> I totally understand the difference between client and server side

"servlets carrying malware" ??

> (and you have mentioned before that JavaScript is client side, when
it can be used
> for both [RUNAT=SERVER]).

Certainly JavaScript can be used as server side, or even as a command
line language. But it will only act as a malware carrier when it is
running on the client.

> A client side script in ANY language that supports access to COM
components on the
> client, CAN be used for malware.

But first it has to get onto the client and be executed there.  You can
put Javascript or VBScript in an HTML page and expect it to execute on
the client because the browser will run it (given appropriate
settings).  You cannot put Perl, Python or PHP in an HTML page and get
it to run on the client because the browser won't run it.  Can't run
it.  It would have to be downloaded and saved by the user and then the
user would have to start the program running.  hardly 'stealth' mode.

> On Non-windows machines the same effect can be obtained with Java
beans on the
> client or using CORBA on a client.

A Java applet in a page from a server is running in a sandbox and has
no access to local resources. It can't write local files or access the
local machine.  It can only link back to the originating server.

If the user downloads, saves, and runs a Java program then that is no
different from him directly downloading and running a .EXE that may be
malware.

> If the server is malicious it can send you a lot more than HTML.

Most of which will be ignored by the browser unless correctly
identified.  The question is about 'stealth mode' where the malware
gets past the user.

> Server push, HTML containing client pull and SSI, are all potentially
harmful,

Excuse me but SSI is _Server_Side Includes.  This is done on the
_server_ and merely puts stuff in the page at the server which could
equally well have been by having it directly in the page. That is, it
is more like a Cobol COPY.   SSI is not 'potentially harmful', the
included code may be, but that is not SSI's fault., the client cannot
know whether SSI was used or not, it never sees this.

Server push or client pull are not _in_themselves_ harmful.  It is the
page content that might be and that can be the same regardless of push,
pull or SSI.

>> It is also an inconvenience to all the mailusers that you may be
>> pumping spam out to.

> But I'm not, so that is just speculation on your part.

I am certain that most of the users of the million (or millions) of
Windows boxes that _are_ pumping out spam would say _exactly_ the same
thing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-05T21:21:30+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bf3l3F6fsqaaU1@individual.net>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1112670347.953014.47280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...
> > No, Java scriptlets and servlets can also carry malware.
>
> Servlets run the server (hence the name) and cannot 'carry' anything to
> your machine.
>
You are showing limited imagination here, Richard. Servlets DO run on the
server but they can formulate HTML and transmit it to your system, and they
can activate programs on other servers to attack ports on the client,
especially once they have its IP adress and therefore know it is active.

> By 'Java "scriplets"' I presiume that you mean applets, which do run on
> the client machine.
>
No, having written both servlets and applets I not only know the difference,
I understand the implications.

> > The fact they they are 'fenced' or 'sandboxed' as you called it does
> not prevent them
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If they are running on a 'malicious server', certainly...

> > (and you have mentioned before that JavaScript is client side, when
> it can be used
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> running on the client.

Again, I say your imagination is limited...
>
> > A client side script in ANY language that supports access to COM
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> user would have to start the program running.  hardly 'stealth' mode.

It is exactly stealth mode if the user is unaware that a port is downloading
malware. The ONLY route is not necessarily via HTML.

I have placed programs onto a server using certain ports and certain
protocols (not http) that the user was completely unaware of ... (it was my
machine and I did it while experimenting with Java Network protocols...
There was no visible sign the transfer had occurred, but it had. All it
needed was a signal to activate it. That is why we (should) have firewalls.

It is also the main reason most sysops disable Telnet until they actually
require it. The protocol I used was not Telnet and I'm really loth to
discuss this too much because it may give some people ideas.

>
> > On Non-windows machines the same effect can be obtained with Java
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That is what is stated in all the Java texts.

I also believed that to be the case until recently. It can be bent.

I had a look around the web to see if there was any actual evidence of this.
I found differing points of view, but there was enough to persuade me that
applets are no more secure than ActiveX.

I think this is the most sensible and non-emotional discussion and it
accurately reflects my own position.

http://www.cs.princeton.edu/sip/faq/java-faq.php3

> If the user downloads, saves, and runs a Java program then that is no
> different from him directly downloading and running a .EXE that may be
> malware.
>
Exactly.

> > If the server is malicious it can send you a lot more than HTML.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not my question...

> > Server push, HTML containing client pull and SSI, are all potentially
> harmful,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You are splitting hairs. I actually agree with you that SSI itself is not
the problem, and it is the content of what is included that causes the
problem, but if SSI was disallowed, it couldn't be included. This is exactly
the whole point that started this debate. If you disable things you can make
the system safer. SSI can do quit a bit more than Server Side Includes, as
its name implies. It can execute programs in some implemenations of it.

> Server push or client pull are not _in_themselves_ harmful.  It is the
> page content that might be and that can be the same regardless of push,
> pull or SSI.
>
I never suggested otherwise. VBScript is not harmful in itself. It depends
on what you write with it.

> >> It is also an inconvenience to all the mailusers that you may be
> >> pumping spam out to.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I can only speak for me. My system doesn't do it and I can prove it doesn't.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2005-04-05T15:06:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2u9he07cm@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net> <1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <3be99sF6hhl2tU1@individual.net> <1112670347.953014.47280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <3bf3l3F6fsqaaU1@individual.net>`

```

In article <3bf3l3F6fsqaaU1@individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> writes:
> "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:1112670347.953014.47280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> especially once they have its IP adress and therefore know it is active.

There's absolutely nothing here that distinguishes servlets from any
other part of the HTTP server.  Any HTTP request can cause arbitrary
actions by the server (or servers) that receives it.  The HTTP server
could "formulate HTML", "activate programs on other servers", and so
forth.  Claiming that this is in some way a property of servlets is
inconsequential; it's a property of everything that runs on the server
side.  PHP can deliver malware and coordinate attacks to precisely the
same extent that any server (including "nc -L") can.

> > By 'Java "scriplets"' I presiume that you mean applets, which do run on
> > the client machine.
> >
> No, having written both servlets and applets I not only know the difference,
> I understand the implications.

So what is a "scriptlet", then?  JSPs?  They're just syntactic sugar
for servlets.  No security difference whatsoever.

> > > I totally understand the difference between client and server side
> >
> > "servlets carrying malware" ??
> >
> If they are running on a 'malicious server', certainly...

How?  The servlet is not downloaded by the client.  It can serve
malware, but so can any other component of the server - a servlet
serving malware is no different from a static link to malware.

And the malware is only effective if the client has an exploitable
vulnerability; and the more capabilities the client has, the more
potential vulnerabilities there are.  That's why client-side
scripting languages are an additional exposure on the client, and
server-side scripting languages are not.

They're an exposure on the server, which may lead to a compromised
server serving malware, but the server-side scripting facility itself
is no more of a malware vector than serving static content is.

> > Certainly JavaScript can be used as server side, or even as a command
> > line language. But it will only act as a malware carrier when it is
> > running on the client.
> 
> Again, I say your imagination is limited...

Malware, or anything else, is delivered to the client as a stream of
octets.  It doesn't matter what generates that stream of octets. It
could be a server-side script; it could be a static file; it could be
hard-coded into the server program itself.  From the client's point
of view it makes no difference whatsoever.

> I have placed programs onto a server using certain ports and certain
> protocols (not http) that the user was completely unaware of ... (it was my
> machine and I did it while experimenting with Java Network protocols...
> There was no visible sign the transfer had occurred, but it had. All it
> needed was a signal to activate it. That is why we (should) have firewalls.

Certainly, but what does that have to do with server-side scripting?

> It is also the main reason most sysops disable Telnet until they actually
> require it. The protocol I used was not Telnet and I'm really loth to
> discuss this too much because it may give some people ideas.

No worries there.  The script kiddies aren't bright enough to use
them, and the real black hats are way ahead of this sort of thing.
They're using side channels in DNS and the like as transports.

Most people disable telnet because they can get a somewhat more
secure remote login mechanism from SSH with little effort.  (As it's
typically used, SSH is still vulnerable to numerous plausible
attacks, because people don't usually practice good key hygiene, but
it provides "kid sister" security, anyway.)  Of course, there are a
variety of secure telnet implementations available, some of them
significantly superior to SSH as it's conventionally used.

Telnet's hardly the major target anyway.  Far more exploits against
Windows systems are tunnelled through DCOM, SMB, and HTTP, which have
traditionally been the gaping holes in Windows network security.

> > A Java applet in a page from a server is running in a sandbox and has
> > no access to local resources. It can't write local files or access the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I also believed that to be the case until recently. It can be bent.

There have been a number of exploitable holes found in Java
sandboxing, some in the core language implementation and some in
particular JVMs.

There have been far, far more exploitable holes found in ActiveX
controls.  In particular, a great number of ActiveX controls marked
"safe for scripting" have, in fact, been proven to be completely
unsafe.

Historically, user agents (such as browsers) which permit ActiveX
executable content have been far more vulnerable than those that
permit only Java executable content.

> I think this is the most sensible and non-emotional discussion and it
> accurately reflects my own position.
> 
> http://www.cs.princeton.edu/sip/faq/java-faq.php3

Sure.  Ed Felten's a good guy (he took on SDMI, after all, which
practically makes him eligible for sainthood in some people's eyes)
and a good security researcher.  And his baseline assessment is
correct: neither ActiveX nor Java should be considered secure.

However, making security decisions from baseline assessments -
especially decisions along the lines of "oh well, we're all in
trouble anyway, so what difference does it make?" - is lousy security
practice.  Good security decisions, the sort that make a difference
in actual security costs and damages, are made based on realistic
threat models and analysis of how various countermeasures mitigate
various threats in it.

And the fact is that the available evidence suggests strongly that
restricting ActiveX prunes a larger portion of the attack tree than
restricting Java does.

Now, I'm not aware of any evidence that demonstrates a similar
disparity between VBScript and Javascript, and I'd be surprised to
see any, since I'm not aware of any significant capability the former
has and the latter lacks, when executed by an HTTP user agent.  I
make it my practice to never execute scripts served by web pages
without confirmation, except for a few internal systems that I trust
(on the grounds that if they're compromised I have much more grievous
exposures to worry about anyway).

> SSI can do quit a bit more than Server Side Includes, as
> its name implies. It can execute programs in some implemenations of it.

But this makes *no difference* to security from the client's point of
view.  Anything done *to the client* by a program executed by SSI or
CGI or any other server-side facility could be equivalently done by
the base server software itself.  The client can't tell the difference.

Server-side scripting has historically been a vulnerability for the
server.  In the early days (say, the NCSA httpd 3.0 era) it was
unsafe sample CGI scripts; then for a while poorly-written Perl
scripts and Visual Basic COM objects with lousy input hygiene; and
these days I see lots of exploits against sloppily-written PHP apps.
And these vulnerabilities lead to server compromises, and a user who
trusts a compromised server may well be compromised in turn.  But
that doesn't make server-side scripting a client-side vulnerability.
```

- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net> <1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <3be99sF6hhl2tU1@individual.net> <1112670347.953014.47280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <3bf3l3F6fsqaaU1@individual.net> <d2u9he07cm@news2.newsguy.com> <3bhq97F6f7pf5U1@individual.net>`

```

In article <3bhq97F6f7pf5U1@individual.net>, "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> writes:
> 
> "Michael Wojcik" <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> servlets.

Fair enough.  I think we're all agreed that any general-purpose
facility for delivering data can deliver data you don't want.  (Even
comp.lang.cobol...)

> I have no issue with the rest of your mail. I never intended to get into a
> discussion of security on either the client or the server, when it is
> blatantly obvious that both are vulnerable.

True, though that's not a sufficient basis for a security policy, which
also has to account for *how* they're vulnerable.

> I guess I got pushed into it when it was suggested that SOME scripting
> languages and environments are safer than others. I simply believe that is a
> subjective decision because ALL of them have vulnerabilities.

Certainly it's subjective in that someone ultimately has to evaluate
risks; we don't have an absolute formal mechanism for that, and even
if we did most people wouldn't use it.  But that doesn't mean that
there are no grounds other than personal opinion on which to make
security decisions.  There's a tremendous body of work on security
risk analysis, with metrics for determining that some decisions are
indeed safer than others under a given rubric.

> It is really silly (and quite dangerous) to get smug about the security of a
> non-MS environment, when the actual statistics show these environments can
> be attacked just as easily as any other.

Agree with the first clause; disagree with the second.  I don't think
anyone's established a satisfactory procedure for determining how
"easily" an environment can be attacked, and analyses of the
statistics we have (which are themselves dubious) are notoriously
inconsistent.

"All environments are vulnerable" is a good security posture; "it
doesn't matter what environment I choose" is not.

> What point is made in saying ActiveX is more dangerous than Java? They are
> BOTH insecure.

The point is that a robust security policy is based on threat model
and countermeasure analysis.  If Java has a narrower attack tree than
ActiveX, then all else being equal, ActiveX *is* more dangerous, and
poses more of a risk to the user.  And "more of a risk" is often a
quantifiable cost in these analyses.  Of course, all else never *is*
equal, but the difference in threat models has to be taken into
account to have a viable security policy.

It's possible to have a good security policy that allows ActiveX, but
it has to account for attack branches which don't exist with Java.
The choice does matter.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-05T14:38:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112737099.979447.299500@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3bf3l3F6fsqaaU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net> <1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <3be99sF6hhl2tU1@individual.net> <1112670347.953014.47280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <3bf3l3F6fsqaaU1@individual.net>`

```
> Servlets DO run on the server but they can formulate HTML
> and transmit it to your system,

No more so than can be in a web page of HTML.   Thay cannot 'formulate
HTML' in a way that is different from what may reside in a file.

> and they  can activate programs on other servers to attack
> ports on the client, especially once they have its IP adress
> and therefore know it is active.

No more so that an IMG tag in a file of HTML can.

> By 'Java "scriplets"' I presiume that you mean applets, which
> do run on the client machine.

> No, having written both servlets and applets I not only know
> the difference, I understand the implications.

Are you using 'Java scriplet' as a term for a JSP - which is merely the
source code that will create a servlet.  If so then JSPs cannot do
anything to the client that cannot be done with ordinary web-page
files.

>> "servlets carrying malware" ??

> If they are running on a 'malicious server', certainly...

But so can an ordinary web-page file.  There is no _extra_ security
implication for the _client_ in JSPs, Servlets, SSI, PHP, Perl or
Python server scripts, or even shell scripts (I have even used DOS
batch files as CGI scripts).  These are merely conveniences for the
developer as far as the content sent to the client is concerned.  They
may do things _on_the_server_ but that is not malicious to the client.

> I have placed programs onto a server using certain ports and
> certain protocols (not http) that the user was completely
> unaware of ... (it was my machine and I did it while
> experimenting with Java Network protocols...

But the port itself will not do anything, it is not a hole into which
you can just stuff code or data and expect it to do anything. It needs,
for example, a server program running on that machine, such as an FTP
server or a Windows NetBIOS responder.

Presumably you had previously installed software onto the server so
that you could send files on that port.

And that is the point about Windows features.  pre-SP2 it could
potentially leave ports open _and_servers_running_ (such as NetBIOS or
RAS) without the user being aware of them.

>> It can't write local files or access the
>> local machine.  It can only link back to the originating server.

> That is what is stated in all the Java texts.

> I also believed that to be the case until recently. It can be bent.
> I found differing points of view, but there was enough to
> persuade me that applets are no more secure than ActiveX.

One difference is that Java Applets are _designed_ to be secure and run
in a sand box so exploits are primarily about bugs. ActiveX is
_designed_ to be able to do anything on the machine and 'security' is
by adding layers.

> it is the content of what is included that causes the problem,
> but if SSI was disallowed, it couldn't be included.

That is like saying "if Cobol disallows COPY of FDs then one couldn't
use files in Cobol".  SSI is merely a convenience to copy in static
fragments or generate some dynamic content - it is often implemented by
mod-perl or similar.

If SSI is disallowed on the server (the client _never_ sees it) then
exactly the same content could be in the web-page files.

> This is
> exactly the whole point that started this debate. If you disable
> things you can make the system safer. SSI can do quit a bit
> more than Server Side Includes, as its name implies. It can
> execute programs in some implemenations of it.

Even the includes are done by running a 'program' which may be part of
the server or an add-on. But it won't run programs on the client.

Your point seemed to be that 'Java scriplets' (JSP), SSI, Perl, Python,
etc running on the _server_ can somehow 'carry' malware to the client
that raw HTML could not.  You are confused.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-06T13:09:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112818192.707514.74940@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3bhrsqF6if34qU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net> <1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <3be99sF6hhl2tU1@individual.net> <1112670347.953014.47280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <3bf3l3F6fsqaaU1@individual.net> <1112737099.979447.299500@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <3bhrsqF6if34qU1@individual.net>`

```
> A server can talk to a client over any one of thousands of ports
using any one
> of hundreds of protocols. Serving HTML on port 80 via http is only
ONE way.

And this has nothing to do with 'scripting' in any form.  It is
irrelevant whether it is hand crafted assembler running inside IIS, an
SSI or a C++ program picking up IP addresses from server logs, or
merely scanning all addresses.

But you are wrong. A server cannot 'talk' to a client over ports using
protocols unless the client has something that specifically listens to
that port and uses that protocol.  If the machine does have, say, an
ftp server daemon running then the server (or in fact _any_ machine
regardless of it being a server or not running _any_ os or language)
can 'talk' to that port.  Sensible configurations of the ftp server
will restrict who can send thing and where they will be stored on the
machine.

So the issue is: will a client machine default to having server
software running that allows other machines (_any_ other machine, not
just SSI on a webserver) to attempt to access without the user being
aware of this. In the case of XP SP2 the answer is probably no.  In the
case of previous iterations the answer is possibly yes.

>> Presumably you had previously installed software onto the server so
>> that you could send files on that port.

> No. The system itself contains such software.

Well exactly.  This was a Windows machine ?  It had software listening
on a port(s) that you could access without the user being aware.
Either the user hadn't bothered to set passwords, or you knew them. In
either case the user is unaware.

> (You know that as well as I do, it is just that you have focused on
what clients and servers
> NORMALLY do rather than what they CAN do.)

And you seem to be rather limited in your imagination of what can be
put in 'normal' HTML on port 80 without server side scripting (or with,
it makes no difference).

> You can place an executable on a machine and ....

Stop right there.  You have started with a conclusion that 'you can
place an executable on a machine'.  You say that as if it can be done
to _any_ machine as easily, or indeed at all.

This, in fact, is one major area where _WINDOWS_ has failed in the
past.  In fact up to SP2, and probably still does.  Not with 'bugs' but
with 'features'.

For example the scrollbar drag-and-drop will 'place an executable' in
the _startup_ area using _features_ of IE and Windows.  This will
execute on the next reboot.

For example a static page of HTML can link to an executable (Windows
.exe or .pif or ..) and this can be disguised by a _feature_ of Windows
such that a file pretty.jpeg.exe looks like pretty.jpeg because Windows
hides the 'type'.  Windows may ask the user whether it is to be saved
to disk or _run_.  The user probably thinks it is a picture.

Other systems _don't_do_this_.  On Linux there is no browser that will
ask if an executable is to be run.  A file saved to disk will _never_
have its 'executable' bit set so it is _not_ an executable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-07T10:14:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bj5ajF684gstU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net> <1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <3be99sF6hhl2tU1@individual.net> <1112670347.953014.47280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <3bf3l3F6fsqaaU1@individual.net> <1112737099.979447.299500@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <3bhrsqF6if34qU1@individual.net> <1112818192.707514.74940@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
Positively last post on this.

Have a look at "Java 2 Network Protocols" Black Book and socket programming.

You do NOT need FTP server or daemon because socket service is inherent, and
not just on Windows systems.
(You don't need a daemon for a server to respond to ping, for instance...
You can configure it not to, but most people don't.)

If the client can accept the protocol (and clients can accept MANY protocols
that users don't even know exist)
then, if proper security measures are not in place, transfer can occur.

How is that 'wrong'?

Pete.

Top Post no more.

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:1112818192.707514.74940@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com...
> > A server can talk to a client over any one of thousands of ports
> using any one
…[65 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-06T16:14:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112829270.556957.191340@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3bj5ajF684gstU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <9U13vlb9flB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3b6oskF6gg8kaU1@individual.net> <9UDItsEuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <3bctk0F6h4ickU1@individual.net> <1112642698.694707.4660@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <3be99sF6hhl2tU1@individual.net> <1112670347.953014.47280@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <3bf3l3F6fsqaaU1@individual.net> <1112737099.979447.299500@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com> <3bhrsqF6if34qU1@individual.net> <1112818192.707514.74940@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <3bj5ajF684gstU1@individual.net>`

```
> You do NOT need FTP server or daemon because socket service is
inherent,
> and not just on Windows systems.
> (You don't need a daemon for a server to respond to ping, for
instance...
> You can configure it not to, but most people don't.)

You _do_ need code to respond to a 'ping', it just happens that this is
provided by the system software. That software will not, however,
'write an executable to the disk' nor will it write any other file.

You _DO_ need _something_ that will not only listen on a particular
port, but will do something with the packets received. Socket Services
will close the port, or simply drop the packet. It may reply with a
'service not available' or not. It will not in itself do anything with
the packet unless a daemon (such as FTP server) tells socket services
(or via the 'services' file) that it will accept packets on that port.

You seem to be implying that Socket Services will somehow, when it
doesn't know what to do with stuff appearing on a random port, will try
writing the packets to disk and then see if it can execute it.

> If the client can accept the protocol (and clients can accept MANY
protocols
> that users don't even know exist)

Given that Windows source code is closed then Windows clients _may_
accept many protocols that no one except MS know even exist. In fact
the MS EULA gives permission to MS to connect to your machine by any
means that have been hidden in Windows and to investigate, to change
software and even delete stuff they don't like without any additional
permission being asked.

It is true that _Windows_ (pre SP2) may accept some protocols that can
write executables to disk and the user was unaware of, such as NetBIOS
shares being exposed to the internet, and RAS and other _features_.

> then, if proper security measures are not in place, transfer can
occur.

Transfer can only occur and result in 'an executable being written to
disk' _IF_ the software on the machine can write to disk.  The software
responding to a 'ping' does not.  An ftp daemon can, an ssh server can,
but these are password protected, even by default, and are restricted
as to where an what they write (eg on Linux the file cannot be written
as being an executable).

> How is that 'wrong'?

Your assumption that if a port is 'open' then this allows a file to be
written to disk using this.

Even if a client side JavaScript program or Java _applet_ (and this is
the only type of Java program that can be loaded into a client browser)
could open a port on the client then it cannot write anything
transferred to disk.

What is wrong is that you confuse what a servlet can do and what an
applet can do when running in a browser.

This is why I prefer OpenSource.  I don't need to read the source code,
or even have it, but I know that someone out there can read it and if,
for example, the 'ping' responder has this weird code that could be
used to 'write an executable' then they would be telling everyone about
it.  With MS all these little secrets and backdoors may be, and
probably are, hidden.

I would not run a Windows machine (nor, preferably, a Linux one either)
except behind a Freesco or Smoothwall firewall or similar* as they
block all ports except those specifically enabled.  With Windows XP
firewalls running on the machine itself I have no idea what MS has in
the underlying configuration (ZoneAlarm I may trust more).


* ADSL _routers_ that I have used have a firewall too, modems do not.
```

```
PD> I use mainly JavaScript. It is pretty simple to use and, although it
PD> has no affiliation with Java, the syntax and concepts are similar.
PD>
PD> I do use VBScript occasionally, mainly because I find it a bit
PD> tiresome to keep 'translating' code samples from VBScript into
PD> JScript.
PD>
PD> It really doesn't matter for WSH, as either or both can be freely
PD> interchanged just as they can on the web.


   There is an interesting article on Microsoft's "Scripting Clinic"  
discussing the choice between JScript and VBScript:
   <http://msdn.microsoft.com/library/en-us/dnclinic/html/vbsvjs.asp>

   Theoretically, WSH (Windows Scriping Host) can accomodate other  
scripting languages, like REXX, Python, or Perl, but I have not yet  
heard of any actual implementations of that.

   As further source of information, I already mentioned the start  
page <http://msdn.microsoft.com/scripting>

   Helpful articles introducing WinScripting in general and some  
applications are to be found at the "Scripting Clinic" at
   <http://msdn.microsoft.com/columns/scripting.asp>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** djreid <djreid@ihug.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-02T23:03:53+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2lu6p$5t2$1@lust.ihug.co.nz>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d2gq7d$nk3$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112229926.139869.22050@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <d2flri$anl$1@panix5.panix.com> <bfceb$424b7b59$45491f85$11785@KNOLOGY.NET> <d2gq7d$nk3$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
below is a batch fel that works with windows 2000 or xp.  May need 
changing for date format.  I have it set for ddmmyyyy.

It use winzips command line addon.  Has include and exclude file

:echo off
cls
::windows xp
::set up dayname,day, month, year in environment variables
::
for /f "tokens=1-4 delims=/ " %%a in ('date /t') do (set weekday=%%a& 
set day=%%b& set month=%%c& set year=%%d)
::
for /f "tokens=1-3 delims=:&. " %%a in ('time /t') do (set hr=%%a& set 
min=%%b& set am=%%c)
::
::use wzzip to make zipfile
::-yp Display a "Press any key to continue" prompt when the operation 
::completes. This
::      is particularly useful when running WZZIP from Start Menu's Run 
::dialog box or from the "start" command.
::-r Recurse into subfolders (include subfolders).  This option requires 
  ::the -p or -P option.
::-p|P	Store folder names.  A lower case p stores only the names of 
::folders recursed (subfolders included)
::        via the -r option, while an upper case P stores all folder 
::information specified on the command line.
::
wzzip -r -P -x@Exclude.txt
C:\backup\zips\AC-%weekday%%year%-%month%-%day%.%hr%.%min%.%am%.zip 
@Include.txt
::
If Not ErrorLevel 1 Goto End
::
Echo ***ERROR DETECTED***
::
echo .
::
pause
:End
cls
::
Xcopy
C:\backup\zips\AC-%weekday%%year%-%month%-%day%.%hr%.%min%.%am%.zip 
"\Documents and Settings\%username%\Local Settings\Application 
Data\Microsoft\CD Burning"
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d2hegl$7r5$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <1112290478.366513.170260@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>,
>  <foodman123@gmail.com> wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…

It can.

I do it all the time. See my earlier post re WSH. (Windows Script Hosting)

I have 4 icons on my destop that run system related procedures using system
utilities and objects. One click, connection is made to a server database
and the database is archived and reorganised.. (It is a MySQL database
located 7000 miles away. No problem, any time, 24/7.) Another one causes the
same server to load a particular test environment that involves baselined
files and databases for testing against known data.  Another click
initialises my own local  host server  (IIS), starts its SMTP mail service,
and opens a portal for incoming remote tests.    All of these would be quite
tedious to run manually.

Despite Dilworth's certainty regarding the superiority of COBOL over
utilities about which he knows nothing, in an XP environment the word
"utility" has a fairly loose definition. Some of these are system services,
some of them are true "utilities" and some of them are system objects with
their own methods and properties.  (You don't need to be an OO expert to
instantiate  a system object and invoke its methods, using script...If you
get stuck I can give you code) All of them are easily controlled by WSH.

Whatever you decide will work, because you are the kind of professional who
will make it work.

The only reason I posted was because it looked like you might be persuaded
to a solution that could only work on Planet Dilworth, and in conscience, I
couldn't do less than my best to prevent that happening to anyone...:-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>

- **In-Reply-To:** `<d2i9t2$3o4$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112303300.336466.13970@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d2i9t2$3o4$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```


docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>,
>  <foodman123@gmail.com> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

We handle backups pretty much the same way we did in 1986.  This is an 
IBM mainframe application with CICS (now Transaction Server 2.x) and 
batch processing.  The CICS files are primarily VSAM, and to maintain 
24x7 availability we have two sets of files, primary and secondary. 
In our overnight maintenance window, CICS processing is switched to 
secondary files.  Primary files are then backed up, using standard 
utilities, IDCAMS and SyncSort, then purged, reloaded, and refreshed. 
  We switch from secondaries back to primaries, and do the same batch 
processing (backup, purge, reload, refresh) to the secondaries.

There is some debate as to whether SyncSort is faster than IDCAMS at 
copying a file, but it doesn't make much difference unless the file is 
really large (multiple gigabytes).  Naturally we back up to "tape", 
but since we now have VTS I cannot be sure when it really goes to 
tape, or to virtual tape.

We now have DB2 tables of course, but the DBA's seem to be in control 
of backing up DB2 tables.

When it comes to backing up my home PC, I'm a bit careless.  I use ZIP 
disks for backup, and rarely use compression.  I have PKZIP 2.50, but 
not WinZip.

For incremental backup, I use a .bat file I wrote:

@echo off
cls
echo ï¿½
echo ****************************************************************
echo * BCOPY.BAT copyright 2004-01-05 by Arnold J. Trembley         *
echo * copy new or changed files/subdirectories from the source     *
echo * directory to the target directory.  The Source parameter     *
echo * may include wildcard characters.                             *
echo ****************************************************************
if "%1"=="" goto ERR01
if "%1"=="/?" goto HELP
if "%2"=="" goto ERR02
goto OK
:err01
echo ï¿½
echo Source directory not specified...
goto help
:err02
echo ï¿½
echo Target directory not specified...
goto help
:help
echo ï¿½
echo %0 -- Backup New/Changed folder to target directory
echo ï¿½
echo Syntax is:  %0 Drive:\Path Drive:\Path
goto NOCOPY
:OK
echo ï¿½
echo XXCOPY %1 %2 /backup /S /H /R /BN
echo ï¿½
xxcopy %1 %2 /backup /S /H /R /BN
goto QUIT
:NOCOPY
echo ï¿½
echo XXCOPY TERMINATED WITHOUT COPYING ANY FILES...
:QUIT
echo ï¿½

This bat file also uses the XXCOPY freeware utility available from:
http://www.xxcopy.com/index.htm

It has a feature where it will bypass copying a file if the source and 
target files have the same date/time stamp.  This is a great time-saver.

I just open a command line window, mount a ZIP disk and type something 
like:  BCOPY \COBOL G:\COBOL
Only the new or changed files are copied to the target directory. 
Backing up in the wrong direction doesn't hurt anything, because of 
the date/time check.

Microsoft RoboCopy also has this feature, but you would have to 
download an entire SDK to get this one utility.  I have a copy from NT 
4.0 that someone gave me, and I believe it does not have any license 
restrictions on sharing.  It works under WinXP home or Professional.

If you type XCOPY /? you will find that the standard XCOPY has a 
similar feature, using the /D option.

I don't require an icon to simplify my home PC backups, so these 
solutions are okay for me.

I don't backup the OS or applications for which I have install CD's. 
Perhaps I am a bit of an optimist this way.  I do make fairly frequent 
backups of data files that are important to me, and multiple copies of 
them.  And periodically I burn them to CD's.

I've been following this discussion with interest, and there are some 
other solutions I might like to try.

With kindest regards,
```

```
How interesting... I used to use a Jaz drive until the Click o' Death 
began eating my disks... oddly enough, this happened right about the time 
read/write CDs became cheap.  I would boot into DOS and use a utility 
named HD95COPY for images to one disk and the WinZip method described 
earlier for the incrementals.

[snip]

>For incremental backup, I use a .bat file I wrote:

something similar, invoking Microsoft's BACKUP in my AUTOEXEC.BAT so that 
once a day, when I turned the machine on (there was a file containing the 
date it wrote to keep it to 'once') it would dump everything that had been 
changed to my B: drive, a state-of-the-art 720K 3 1/2" floppy.

[snip]

>similar feature, using the /D option.

After backing up with HD95COPY I would use xcopy c:\*.* e: /s /m to turn 
off the attribute bytes before rebooting Win95; I found it to be faster 
than attrib.

[snip]

>other solutions I might like to try.

and this tenet is so Basic it needs neither dusting nor examining.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2005-04-02T10:48:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <NO33e.29754$cg1.28994@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
> Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> opinions on how this should be done differ?

Yes, I noticed that.  I'm sure he is satisfied with his approach, and 
my shop is likewise comfortable with our backup procedures.  Isn't it 
a wonderful world where there is a multitude of ways to do things?

> [snip]
>>I use ZIP 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> earlier for the incrementals.

I have read about the click of death and Iomega drives, but I have yet 
to encounter it.  I have two ZIP 250meg drives, but I tend to use only 
the USB-powered one, switching it between my desktop and laptop as 
needed.

I have not yet gotten around to trying out CD-RW's.  The CD-R's I burn 
are permanent backups, and I find the ZIP disk backups seem to take 
less time than burning a CD.

> [snip]
> After backing up with HD95COPY I would use xcopy c:\*.* e: /s /m to turn 
> off the attribute bytes before rebooting Win95; I found it to be faster 
> than attrib.

I have never found the "Archive" bit useful.  When I backup my files, 
I like to make multiple copies.  If the archive bit is reset, I can't 
make a second, third, or fourth copy of the same files.

> [snip]
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

You're quite welcome.  And it was worth twice what you paid for it!

With kindest regards,
```
