```
I had a Zip100 external (parallel) and a Jaz 1G internal... and after 
about six or seven years of nigh-constant use they both went COD.  I 
figured that was a sign for me to move on.

>
>I have not yet gotten around to trying out CD-RW's.  The CD-R's I burn 
>are permanent backups, and I find the ZIP disk backups seem to take 
>less time than burning a CD.

Time doesn't seem to be a major constraint under my present setup.  Weekly 
incrementals take about a half-hour to write and maybe twenty minutes to 
verify, daily incrementals are a minute or three... but I don't have any 
major development work on my PC or large databases to deal with.

>
>> [snip]
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>make a second, third, or fourth copy of the same files.

There we differ... once and away and that is it, for me.  When something 
goes 'kerflooie' I just restore last week's system and the dailies.

[snip]

>> 
>> Thanks much.
>> 
>
>You're quite welcome.  And it was worth twice what you paid for it!

Even more, two and a quarter... but sometimes one gets a Real Bargain.

DD
```

```
(cartoon figure of a pussy cat)

Thought
you'd like a funny
card for your
birthday, but
what I think is
funny and what YOU
think is funny may be
very different.

For instance -

(now open card)

I think THIS
is funny

(reflective mirror)

What do YOU think ? !
Happy Birthday !

----- if you don't hear from me subsequent to Monday - you will *know* 
it didn't work.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2005-04-02T17:31:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2mktt028g1@news2.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112303300.336466.13970@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d2i9t2$3o4$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

In article <d2i9t2$3o4$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com writes:
> In article <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>,
>  <foodman123@gmail.com> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> unacceptable?

Well, that's what mirroring is for, and I think it's reasonable to
include mirroring in the category of "backups".

However, I agree that Tony's dicta regarding backups fail to take
into account the wide range of data to be backed up, conditions under
which it is created, and conditions under which it is restored.

For example, only ever taking incremental backups (which is implied
by "not be backed up further if [it] is never changed") makes
restoration infeasible under many conditions (too many incrementals
to restore), and leaves you at the mercy of the integrity of the
original full backup.

Waiting until "final compilation" to back up source code is just
silly.  In my book, source should be regularly (eg daily and before
any major changes) checked into a change management system, and the
system's repository should be backed up daily.  With change
management there's no reason to tie backup to the state of the
source.

And so on.  There is no perfect backup regimen which serves all
purposes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2005-04-02T14:37:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2ms9c$k28$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d2i9t2$3o4$1@panix5.panix.com> <d2mktt028g1@news2.newsguy.com>`

```
In article <d2mktt028g1@news2.newsguy.com>,
Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
>
>In article <d2i9t2$3o4$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com writes:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>include mirroring in the category of "backups".

I do not believe Mr Dilworth, in his emphasis on a COBOL application to do 
the simultaneous backup, was considering mirroring, which I understand to 
be more a matter of systems than applications... but my beliefs have been 
proven wrong in the past, sure.

>
>However, I agree that Tony's dicta regarding backups fail to take
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>original full backup.

... hence the mantra of 'Incremental Daily, System Weekly'.  At the very 
least the system backup is verified against the source data.

[snip]

>And so on.  There is no perfect backup regimen which serves all
>purposes.

What... no 'one size fits all'?  I'm not sure Mr Dilworth agrees with 
that... but I suppose he can speak for himself.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2005-04-02T20:47:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%WH3e.27916$w63.1791844@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<d2ms9c$k28$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d2i9t2$3o4$1@panix5.panix.com> <d2mktt028g1@news2.newsguy.com> <d2ms9c$k28$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <d2mktt028g1@news2.newsguy.com>,
> Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is damned silly.  There is a place for both.

Example: A transaction processing style of system, the norm in financial 
type systems, is going to store incoming transactions in such a manner 
that they can be re-applyed to a starting file (presumably the last 
backed up version of a data base).

Backup of a production system is *always* a combination of internal 
methods that make re-creation possible, and a system type of backup that 
is going to physically re-create data for storage.

If you only use one or the other, then  likely you are not doing it right.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2005-04-04T16:43:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2rqqs01v1r@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d2i9t2$3o4$1@panix5.panix.com> <d2mktt028g1@news2.newsguy.com> <d2ms9c$k28$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

In article <d2ms9c$k28$1@panix5.panix.com>, docdwarf@panix.com writes:
> In article <d2mktt028g1@news2.newsguy.com>,
> Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> the simultaneous backup, was considering mirroring,

I don't have any reason to suspect that he was.  I was merely noting
that mirroring is a suitable approach to such a situation, and one
could reasonably count that part of a "backup" process.  If I were to
ask someone how they backed up some data, and they mentioned mirroring,
I would consider that a relevant response.  (If that were the *only*
thing they mentioned, I'd also consider it insufficient, but that's
another point.)

> which I understand to be more a matter of systems than applications...

That's a reasonable distinction, and in a discussion of backups you
might reasonably specify that you're confining your position to
application-level backups.  I merely wanted to note that technology
exists to keep multiple copies of data that is changed very
frequently.

> >And so on.  There is no perfect backup regimen which serves all
> >purposes.
> 
> What... no 'one size fits all'?  I'm not sure Mr Dilworth agrees with 
> that...

I'd guess not, based on his previous arguments.  (I wasn't attempting
to defend his position, in any event.)

> but I suppose he can speak for himself.

History would suggest that, yes.
```

- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112303300.336466.13970@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <d2i9t2$3o4$1@panix5.panix.com> <d2mktt028g1@news2.newsguy.com> <2458f$424f7796$45491f85$4525@KNOLOGY.NET>`

```
In article <2458f$424f7796$45491f85$4525@KNOLOGY.NET>, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> writes:
> 
> Hear, hear - I had two completed project (finished up while on vacation) 
> on my laptop that was stolen towards the end of the second week.  (Also 
> had all our digital pictures...)

Ow.  You have my sympathies.

> Who thinks of backups on vacation?  I know one guy in Alabama that does now!

Just a few weeks back I was out of the office attending a course and
my laptop hard drive suffered a fatal crash.  I was eventually able
to get most of the data off of it (by booting off a CD-ROM into
Windows PE - which is something every Windows user ought to have, by
the way; I think anyone can download it free of charge from Microsoft),
but for a while I didn't know what would and would not be recoverable.
Travel does put laptops at risk, but with all the other things that
need attending to during a trip it's the easiest time to neglect
backing up.
```

```
> I was eventually able to get most of the data off of it (by booting
off a
> CD-ROM into Windows PE - which is something every Windows user
> ought to have,

Some have used a Knoppix CD to boot a machine with a broken Windows
drive as Knoppix will mount FAT32 and NTFS partitions and will cater
for copying any usable files off to, say, a USB drive.  Several other
distros will do much the same.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2005-04-05T13:58:03+00:00

```
> > I was eventually able to get most of the data off of it (by booting

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> distros will do much the same.

Yes, Knoppix would very likely have worked as well.  (Years ago it
was sometimes difficult to get some laptop network cards working with
Linux drivers, due to odd hardware tricks played by laptop designers,
but that no longer seems to be an issue.)  I used WinPE because I had
it handy.  I recommend it for Windows users because it's familiar to
them; for someone comfortable with Linux, Knoppix might be a superior
choice, as there are more tools available on the typical Knoppix disk
than the typical WinPE one.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<1112382405.824284.13500@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1112327351.693618.240810@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112276632.989130.67710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d2h2a9$h5j$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112290478.366513.170260@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1112297206.712860.75980@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1112303300.336466.13970@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1112327351.693618.240810@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com>`

```


> A file should be backed-up immediately after it has been
> changed in some way.  It should not be backed up further
> if is never changed.


> That may be fine for tiny, trivial and/or single-user systems, in the

> case of interactive systems there many be many users entering data
that
>  are changing many files almost continuously.  In fact if there is
one
> process reading through a file and writing out a copy it may well be
> that it fails to capture related data correctly.

Obviously, no one is going to backup,  for example, a POS
data file during business  hours when it is constantly being changed.
Such files would be  backed up at the end of business.

My point is that if a user changed a Price File before the
day's business began then that file should be backed up as
soon as they finish making changes.  That was applicable
20 years ago and is applicable today
and will be applicable tomorrow.

Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T12:55:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112388903.638104.223880@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1112382405.824284.13500@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112276632.989130.67710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d2h2a9$h5j$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112290478.366513.170260@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1112297206.712860.75980@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1112303300.336466.13970@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1112327351.693618.240810@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <1112382405.824284.13500@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
> Obviously, no one is going to backup,  for example, a POS
> data file during business  hours when it is constantly being changed.
> Such files would be  backed up at the end of business.

In one POS system that I have worked with the sales data is stored
locally _and_ updates the server.  This allows the POS terminal to
continue automonously if there is any failure in the rest of the system
and it provides a backup of the days transactions.

Each system should be designed to meet the _users_ actual requirements,
not what the developer thinks of.

> My point is that if a user changed a Price File before the
> day's business began then that file should be backed up as
> soon as they finish making changes.  That was applicable
> 20 years ago and is applicable today and will be applicable tomorrow.


That may well be completely true of _your_ systems, because that is how
they work, and they seem to run on unreliable systems, but that does
not make your particular mechanism a necessity in others. For example
it may be that prices can be updated at times other than 'before the
day's business', or the business may be 24 hour, or there may be
scheduled changes that are done automatically without the user being
present.

Others may have _much_better_ solutions, such as database replication
and automatic failover.  This need not be expensive - a couple of PCs
with journalised file systems, RAID, PostgreSQL and Slony and UPS can
provide uninteruptable service even when hardware fails.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T13:50:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112392229.722196.181100@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1112382405.824284.13500@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112276632.989130.67710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d2h2a9$h5j$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112290478.366513.170260@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1112297206.712860.75980@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1112303300.336466.13970@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1112327351.693618.240810@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <1112382405.824284.13500@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`

```
> That was applicable 20 years ago and is applicable today
> and will be applicable tomorrow.

Just over 20 years ago PCs were mostly single-user floppy disk
machines. MS-DOS was a jumped up version of CP/M that had the inferior
FAT file system from a 1977 BASIC machine strapped to it.  Executing
utilities from a Cobol program usually resulted in running out of
memory and may result in open files being corrupted even if the rather
poor SHARE was loaded in later versions.

Yes, checking files after every update and backing up was entirely
applicable to those systems. Writing your own programs rather than
attempting to execute utilities under Cobol control was sensible, even
essential.  If you are still using DOS and/or FAT then that will still
be applicable because you will still have a crap system. Your advice
may even be 'better' for anyone else using DOS and/or FAT.

However, most of us are no longer stuck in a time warp that requires
such mechanisms. I did use CP/M (and MP/M) and other 8 bit systems in
the late 70s/early 80s and know what most of the limitations were, I
avoided MS-DOS entirely, but used (non-MS) DOS based multiuser systems
and Unix which had reliable file systems and usable command line
utilities that could do things in background or automatically and
didn't have the problems from 20 years ago that you still try to avoid.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-05T01:32:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3bctvuF6fi7khU1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112276632.989130.67710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d2h2a9$h5j$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112290478.366513.170260@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1112297206.712860.75980@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1112303300.336466.13970@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no> <1112308317.534253.171720@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <1112327351.693618.240810@o13g2000cwo.googlegroups.com> <1112382405.824284.13500@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <4250A24A.70705@optonline.net>`

```

"Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote in message
news:4250A24A.70705@optonline.net...
>
> Is transaction logging, and roll back part of backup?
>

My vote would be "No" but it is arguable.

Whether an inflight transaction succeeds or not is a function of the
transaction processing, and it is the responsibility of such software to
leave all databases and stores in the state they were in before the failed
transaction started.  Roll back therefore occurs immediately when the
transaction fails and before it is committed. That is simply transaction
processing, not backup.

You could certainly use transaction logs as part of an overall backup
strategy, but in and of themselves, I would not consider these logs to be
backup.

Pete.
```

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no...
> foodman123@gmail.com wrote:
> > Rather than quibble about whether utilities can do  things which a
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> I think there is some merit to Tony's approach.

Well, go and lie down in a darkened room until you're feeling better.

His solution would require his backup program to be deployed to every
machine running his application, when these machines already contain
perfectly adequate utilities for doing this. And every new application would
need another specific, non-standard, backup program. This is one of the
reasons that led to the downfall of COBOL: a closed file system that nothing
else can get to. Data that is simply 'unshareable' without specific COBOL
programs to access it, when every other application in the system can use
utilities to back up. (Do you think the need for extra COBOL programs to
backup the system would make it more attractive to the buyer?) Not only
that, but, as so eloquently pointed out by Richard, his COBOL backup
strategy is flawed anyway...


> Consider - way back
> using a DOS version of Winzip, I would periodically winzip my
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Most of the Professional IT people I've encountered (and their number is
legion), actually verify backups before filing them. And although WinZip
purports to be an "archiving" utility only a brave man would use it as such.

For home use, OK.

Even for home use, I value my data enough to image copy to a duplicate
external disk drive. (It used to be  20 Gigs over Firewire using  a PCMCIA
card, now it is 60 Gigs over USB through a high speed hub ...(the 60 GB
actually takes the same time as the 20 used to... ah, technology.... it will
be interesting to see what cultural differences will be stimulated should it
ever reach Planet Dilworth...)

If the unthinkable was to happen, 10 minutes with a screwdriver should have
me back on the air after a quick hard drive swap. Then the incrementals from
(uncompressed) CD...

Because you had a bad experience with non-current software, used in a casual
manner, does not in any way make Dilworth's suggestion more valuable.

Now go and sin no more... :-)

Pete.
```

```
>His solution would require his backup program to be deployed to every
>machine running his application, when these machines already contain
>perfectly adequate utilities for doing this.

So what is wrong with that.  The backup program is PART OF the
application so it is there anyway. It is obviously better for the user
to be able to do everything from any machine.

All of my customers with networks only install the software on the
server
and not on 'every machine running the application'. What do you do?

> And every new application would
>need another specific, non-standard, backup program.

And what is wrong with that.  Having written one, that is the skeleton
for all others. I mean,  you are a COBOL programmer, aren't you?

> (Do you think the need for extra COBOL programs to
>backup the system would make it more attractive to the buyer?)

No, it is not intended to make it more attractive (although it does)
it is intended to make it more dependable and easier to use.


> Not only
>that, but, as so eloquently pointed out by Richard, his COBOL backup
>strategy is flawed anyway...

Can you be really, really specific about the flaws, please. Thank you.

(May I suggest that your 'Red Mist' clouds your vision?)


Tony Dilworth
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T13:21:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112390508.139677.95110@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<1112383254.964414.151930@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112276632.989130.67710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d2h2a9$h5j$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112290478.366513.170260@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1112297206.712860.75980@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1112303300.336466.13970@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no> <3b512qF5pkri4U1@individual.net> <1112383254.964414.151930@l41g2000cwc.googlegroups.com>`

```
> No, it is not intended to make it more attractive (although it does)
> it is intended to make it more dependable and easier to use.

It is entirely possible to make a Cobol system where selecting from the
user menu (as you seem to do) _may_ result in a Cobol program running,
_or_ a utility or shell script running, or it may start a background
process and then return.

The way the user starts the backup does not (as you seem to think)
determine how the backup should execute.

Now I certainly agree that the backup should not be a mouse clicking
and keying exercise for the user navigating through some strange GUI.
It should be automatic with as little user
interaction as possible (given they will need to change media).

So your point that 'it is on the user menu just like other programs' is
completely null and void, so can any other mechanism.

As for 'dependable', having the user need to action it makes it
undependable (or should that be 'pendable'). Automatic, scheduled,
overnight backups that report via EMail may be preferable.  Transaction
journalising or replication should make it even _easier_ because there
is nothing to do, nothing to forget.
```

```
would have made that very clear.

Your use of the prase 'what is wrong with that'  indicates there is little
point in my responding, even if I was inclined to, which I'm not. If you
can't see 'what is wrong with that', then it is a waste of time us trying to
have a 'conversation'.


> > (Do you think the need for extra COBOL programs to
> >backup the system would make it more attractive to the buyer?)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And it doesn't.

>
> > Not only
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

So you can say 'And what is wrong with that'? I don't think so...


> (May I suggest that your 'Red Mist' clouds your vision?)
>

Absolutely. No doubt about it. I tend to get misty about people who publicly
 accuse me of lying, never retract what they have said, even when it is
proven to be untrue, but most of all, who throw down a challenge promising
certain forfeits if they lose, then, when they are convincingly seen to have
lost, decline. (I believe it involved you standing in a shop window
somewhere in New York if the forum decided my coding style was more to their
liking than yours.) Apretty stupid thing to bet on, but you did it.

Personally, I really don't care about the opinions on COBOL code and coding
styles, but I would certainly fly to New York city to photograph you doing a
forfeit for your arrogance.

OK, here's the bottom line.

I have not (and it is extremely unlikely that I EVER will (not being a
Christian, I don't believe in forgiveness without some kind of amends being
made... )), gotten over the things you have done in the past, and for which
you have never expressed the slightest contrition.

I think you are an arrogant prick who lives in a time warp and inflicts it
on others at every opportunity. I still haven't forgetten the Dilworth
method for dealing with job applicants...

I'm not even sure if you are on the same planet as me (I bloody well hope
not...)

And even responding to you demeans me. So I won't in future.

Yeah, I know... it's only COBOL  and it's only CLC and I should let it go.

But the mist clouds my perspective.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** foodman123@gmail.com
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T13:12:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112387093.381168.257340@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3b512qF5pkri4U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112276632.989130.67710@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <d2h2a9$h5j$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112290478.366513.170260@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <1112297206.712860.75980@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com> <1112303300.336466.13970@g14g2000cwa.googlegroups.com> <33_2e.860613$Xk.156645@pd7tw3no> <3b512qF5pkri4U1@individual.net>`

```
>His solution would require his backup program to be deployed to every
>machine running his application, when these machines already contain
>perfectly adequate utilities for doing this.

In my systems, the backup program is an integral part of the
application.
So, wherever the application is installed the backup is there too.

Further, all of my network users install the software only on the
server
rather than 'every machine running (the) application". What do you do?


> And every new application would
>need another specific, non-standard, backup program

And what is wrong with that?  Please advise.

The backup program is specific to the application. Besides, having made
the
first, the making of the second and subsequent ones is trivial
for a competent COBOL programmer.

>(Do you think the need for extra COBOL programs to
>backup the system would make it more attractive to the buyer?)

No, it is not intended to make it more attractive, (although it does
that)
but to make it more dependable and easier to manage files.

> but, as so eloquently pointed out by Richard, his COBOL backup
>strategy is flawed anyway...

Would you kindly be really, really specific and detail the flaws for me
so
that I may address them?  Thank you.

Tony Dilworth
```

```
> Actually, if I use, say, a COPY utility, then the file is still
> readable by the Cobol program. Or even if I tar or zip and untar or
> unzip then the file is readable.

I'm sorry you don't get the point. The point is that if you back up the
file with a COBOL program, then you know for sure that it is OK.
The utility will merely backup the file even if it is damaged .

> You can easily imagine a situation where due to some glitch or other
> a data file had some of its sectors overwritten.


> No, I can't image such a situation.  I may imagine that may have
> happened with MS-DOS a couple of decades ago on floppy disks, but
such
> things haven't been seen with modern systems.

Well, then you lead a charmed life.  From time to time over the past
few
years, I have encountered source files which had been partly
overwritten.
This has happened to me with XP which, is I think, a modern system.
Am I the only person in the world to suffer this, I think not.

Tony Dilworth
```

```
> From time to time over the past few years, I have encountered source
files which had been
> partly overwritten.

Then you are using a very poor operating system or bad hardware.

> This has happened to me with XP which, is I think, a modern system.

I certainly don't think of XP as a reliable system, were you using
FAT32 by any chance ?  FAT was designed for floppy disks nearly 30
years ago and was never very good at those. NTFS is alleged to be a
journalising file system, but I have my doubts about that.
```

```
> I'm sorry you don't get the point. The point is that if you back up
the
> file with a COBOL program, then you know for sure that it is OK.
> The utility will merely backup the file even if it is damaged .

If you have a system in which writing a file may result in corruption
of the data or loss of parts of the file then you cannot rely on the
backup file being undamaged when written even if reading the original
showed that to be perfect.

Restoring from that damaged backup then results in loss of data.
```

```
over things that you admit you have no knowledge of.
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] Windows/Macro Language Info?

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2005-04-04T00:50:32+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ba74vF6hgmo6U1@individual.net>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
Since this thread started I have had several private mails requesting more
information on controlling the windows environment with Windows Scripting
Host.

I've therefore prepared a "Hosting 101" pack which explains the concepts,
gives two sample (fully annotated and explained) scripts that deal with
backing up data, and provides links to further information.

I think most COBOL people should be able to handle it, but I'll gladly
assist anyone who requests it.

The 'pack' is three files (2 scripts and an 8 page Word document) all
combined as a ZIP document.

I'm putting it in the public domain with the following provisos:

1. No responsibility for consequential or any other kind of damage if anyone
screws up while scripting.
2. You will need the Windows Operating system (32 bit and preferably (but
not essentially) XP) to run the samples.
3. You will need MS WORD (an edition above word '97) to read the document
properly. (I believe a free download can be obtained of a word document
reader, from MS).
4. You will need WinZIP 8.0 or better to unzip the pack.

You can get it from this link: http://taurangawriters.org.nz

Click on the "Tech Help" button  on the home page and you will be offered a
download. It is less than 30K.

Pete.

TOP POST - nothing new below.

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com...
>
> All righty, then... not COBOL-related but maybe someone might be able to
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
their IP address or email address will be grabbed, can rest easy... No
details of accesses are stored except an indirect count of them. Server log
is recycled every 100 entries. I just want people to get to grips with this
stuff; I'm not interested in selling them anything, or passing on their
details for profit.)

offer"... say, 1 month.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** l.willms@jpberlin.de (Lueko Willms)
- **Date:** 2005-04-04T14:46:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9UDLC8peflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de>`
- **References:** `<d2eaim$r9b$1@panix5.panix.com> <1112555886.165678.107560@z14g2000cwz.googlegroups.com> <3bbhspF6i6eclU1@individual.net>`

```
.    On  04.04.05
  wrote  dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Pete Dashwood)
     on  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
     in  3bbhspF6i6eclU1@individual.net
  about  Re: Windows/Macro Language Info?


PD> Well, I'm pleased to see that around a dozen people have already
PD> grabbed it. (It makes my time worthwhile... :-))

   I've also downloaded it. Thanks for you own short introduction to  
Windows Scripting, which I am currently reading. Besides that, I  
recommend to download the full documentation on the WSH 5.6 from  
http://msdn.microsoft.com/scripting

   As to MsgBx which you described in your document -- I was surprised  
not to find a corresponding function in JScript.

   But there is something else available: Popup, a method of a WShell  
object:


-- popup.js --- schnipp -----------------------------------------

var WshShell = WScript.CreateObject("WScript.Shell");
var BtnCode = WshShell.Popup("Do you feel alright?", 7, "Answer This Question:", 4 + 32);
switch(BtnCode) {
   case 6:
      WScript.Echo("Glad to hear you feel alright.");
      break;
   case 7:
      WScript.Echo("Hope you're feeling better soon.");
      break;
   case -1:
      WScript.Echo("Is there anybody out there?");
      break;
}

-- popup.vbs ---- schnipp -----------------------------------------

Dim WshShell, BtnCode
Set WshShell = WScript.CreateObject("WScript.Shell")

BtnCode = WshShell.Popup("Do you feel alright?", 7, "Answer This Question:", 4 + 32)

Select Case BtnCode
   case 6      WScript.Echo "Glad to hear you feel alright."
   case 7      WScript.Echo "Hope you're feeling better soon."
   case -1     WScript.Echo "Is there anybody out there?"
End Select

------------------ schnapp --------------------------------

   The message box is displayed for at most 7 seconds (2nd parameter);  
-1 is returned on timeout. With 0 as second parameter, it would wait  
till a button is 'pressed'.

   The examples are taken from the Windows Scripting documentation.


   A further remark: it is not necessary to build a WSH file with its  
XML-Structure; it may simply be a pure Javascript or pure VBScript  
file with .JS or .VBS file name extensions.


Yours,
Lï¿½ko Willms                                     http://www.willms-edv.de
/--------- L.WILLMS@jpberlin.de -- Alle Rechte vorbehalten --

A. Der Mann hat viele Kinder. B. Ja, aber ich glaube, von den meisten hat er bloï¿½ die Korrektur besorgt.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
```

```
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, Lueko, I know. I mentioned in the document that JScript could achieve
it by using the OS windowing (that is Shell and Popup)

As it was a 101 type intro. and I wanted to describe both VBScript and
JScript and the use of both interchangeably, I opted not to use popup.


>
> -- popup.js --- schnipp -----------------------------------------
>
> var WshShell = WScript.CreateObject("WScript.Shell");
> var BtnCode = WshShell.Popup("Do you feel alright?", 7, "Answer This
Question:", 4 + 32);
> switch(BtnCode) {
>    case 6:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> BtnCode = WshShell.Popup("Do you feel alright?", 7, "Answer This
Question:", 4 + 32)
>
> Select Case BtnCode
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Which are also referenced in the document.

>
>    A further remark: it is not necessary to build a WSH file with its
> XML-Structure; it may simply be a pure Javascript or pure VBScript
> file with .JS or .VBS file name extensions.
>

Not if you intend it to be transportable and to run under the Windows
Scripting Host, which is what I am talking about. Yes scripts can run as
pure VBScript or JavaScript (if you have these facilities enabled) but you
can then only use the facilities available in that specific environment. WSH
allows you to 'mix and match'. And there is much more besides which I didn't
cover and have no intention of covering. I spent months going through the MS
docs and I am pretty familiar with the classes and objects available. It is
staggeringly comprehensive and effectively opens up the Operating System and
the hard drive to the user. (That is why some people consider it dangerous.
I see that as a separate discussion.)  I can't cover it in a single
document, even if I wanted to.

If you got something form the download, I am content... :-)

Pete.

>
> Yours,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> A. Der Mann hat viele Kinder. B. Ja, aber ich glaube, von den meisten hat
er blo� die Korrektur besorgt.  -G.C.Lichtenberg
>
```

<a id="t-29145a72aa"></a>
## Thread: OT: Looking for someone to help me. Starting a career in an English-speaking country — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Looking for someone to help me. Starting a career in an English-speaking country

- **From:** "Tim Cordsen" <tim_cordsen@gmx.de>
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T09:11:25+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<424cf414$0$27200$9b4e6d93@newsread4.arcor-online.net>`

```
Hi,

my name is Tim, a short introduction is given in: 
http://groups.google.de/groups?hl=de&lr=&threadm=3aenqaF6boeucU1% "Cobol Job 
needed" in this Group.

It would be great if someone could tell me which documents I have to send to 
a possible employer. Which of these documents have to be in English, which 
may stay in German?
Does anyone have an application, a curriculum vitae and a profile for me as 
an example?

Thanks so far,

your

Tim
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Looking for someone to help me. Starting a career in an English-speaking country

- **From:** "Robert Jones" <rjones0@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T09:31:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1112376665.909177.15930@f14g2000cwb.googlegroups.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<424cf414$0$27200$9b4e6d93@newsread4.arcor-online.net>`
- **References:** `<424cf414$0$27200$9b4e6d93@newsread4.arcor-online.net>`

```

Tim Cordsen wrote:
> Hi,
>
> my name is Tim, a short introduction is given in:
> http://groups.google.de/groups?hl=de&lr=&threadm=3aenqaF6boeucU1%
"Cobol Job
> needed" in this Group.
>
> It would be great if someone could tell me which documents I have to
send to
> a possible employer. Which of these documents have to be in English,
which
> may stay in German?
> Does anyone have an application, a curriculum vitae and a profile for
me as
> an example?
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Tim


Hello Tim,

I am not an employment advisor, but it seems to me that you need to
send a CV in English to the prospective employer with a covering letter
to ask them what other documents are required.  Individual companies
may have differing requirements.

It is not a good idea to send originals of certificates, often it is
possible to get certificated copies or translations.

You could also get advice from the embassies of the countries you are
interested in and perhaps from your own government's employment
agencies.

It might cost you but approaching a reputable private employment agency
with a presence in both your country and the one you are interested in
working could also be a good idea.

Good luck, Robert
```

<a id="t-0a802f4556"></a>
## Thread: COBOL imports and exports — 2 msgs

### COBOL imports and exports

- **From:** steve.keanie@rogers.com (Steve Keanie)
- **Date:** 2005-04-01T08:29:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e7007891.0504010829.48b84563@posting.google.com>`

```
Does anyone know if there's a COBOL equivalent to the Enterprise PL/I
SYSDEFSD DD statement (_not_ the binder SYSDEFSD) ? The PL/I compiler
writes information like the following to that file:

EXPORTS CODE
  B042080
  B04208B
  B04208A
IMPORTS
  UCM0030
  UCM0050
  UCM0130
  UCM0430
  UTB0004

Thanks in advance,
Steve Keanie
Ministry of Transport, Ontario
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL imports and exports

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2005-04-07T03:36:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VU15e.7430208$f47.1366994@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<e7007891.0504010829.48b84563@posting.google.com>`

```
Not certain if this will work - and it certainly isn't "self-evident" but I 
*THINK* that the SYSADATA information includes what you want.

Start at:
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg20/APPENDIX1.7.10

and/or check the COBOL books for SYSADATA and ADATA.
```

```
> Every woman seeking an abortion should be given an ultrasound, and be shown 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

<sarcastic reply, not serious>

And every women who decideds to "keep" her pregnancy and place her child up for 
"adoption" should be shown pictures of (bad) group homes, over-filled foster 
homes, and children who run away from abusive foster/adoptive parents.

Parents of (diagnosed) defective children should be shown pictures of what 
happens to similar children when their parents die (good and bad).

Women pregnant after rape should be shown pictures of divorce courts processing 
"broken" marriages when non-biological farthers can't handle such situations.

  ***

Non-sarcastic reply,
   It would be "really nice" if the US (and/or other countries) actually could 
come up with a medically defensible (and probably subject to change) LEGAL 
definition of when "life begins".  It would be REALLY interesting if this 
applied to ALL things (such as how old you are to get a drivers licens, vote, 
drink, get social-security, etc).

Let me be VERY clear, I do NOT have such a definition.  It seems "clear" to me 
that life definitely begins (or has begun) when a baby is "born" (separated 
physically and continues to live) from her/his mother. The problem arises when a 
fetus is NOT separated but has the "potential" to survive separation from 
his/her mother (both with and without medical device assistance).  For me, 
personally, (and I KNOW that this is not universally accepted) when a fetus is 
at a stage that it canNOT survive (even with help) outside the mother's womb, 
then the fetus may be protected by OTHER things, but not by such concepts 
applying to the "lving" such as murder.  To ME, "potential human" is NOT the 
same thing as "current human".
```

```
> And every women who decideds to "keep" her pregnancy and place her child

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I think that there is more than a hint of honesty in there.  Fact is that 
there are any number of situations where there are bad things that come out 
of birth.  I generally don't get into abortion debates because I feel it is 
an intensely private decision that has so many factors I don't think that my 
judgment has any merit at all.  Some people are equipped to deal with more 
than others - and I don't think it's my place to judge them.  Some people 
have religion to help  them with these decisions, some people don't.  Some 
people have a strong family support structure, some people don't.

I do think the general statement that "abortion as birth control" is 
overblown because it is major surgery - it's not a simple procedure and it's 
more emotionally complicated.  I feel more saddened by anyone needing to 
make that choice rather than the choice itself.

That said I often here people say "what do they do? they have no money and 
they have no food and all they do is screw and have babies" about poor 
countries and locations....well, what we do about this? Preach 
abstinence...that has a *great* track record.

> Non-sarcastic reply,
>   It would be "really nice" if the US (and/or other countries) actually 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> licens, vote, drink, get social-security, etc).
Life begins at 18, when you get to go to college ;)
<facetious>
However, generally speaking, life is that time between birth and death.  How 
more simple can it get ?
</facetious>

> Let me be VERY clear, I do NOT have such a definition.  It seems "clear" 
> to me that life definitely begins (or has begun) when a baby is "born" 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> "current human".

The issue with your definition is that soon a phoetus will be grown in a jar 
in a lab and your argument has ethical as well as a moral argument.  I'd be 
interested to see what the concensus is once we get closer to that stage. 
Right now it is strictly a moral argument.

I personally don't understand why we don't have more fuss about fertility 
treatments and test tube babies- if you cannot have kids, get over it, get a 
dog, adopt do something else with your life.

Obviously God didn't want you to have them....but WAIT, we have fertility 
treatment that will give you TRIPLETS.....well done.   Science is allowed to 
create life, but not "take it away".   I like that.  Everything is good 
about the world.

JCE
```

```
was borrowed?
What if the mother was a lesbian?
What about the notion that God is the creator of life and death?  Isn't that 
sovereignty threatened by artificial means of messing with conception?

My understanding from the Bible is that there were many barren women and 
each would eventually give birth after *prayer* to God.  Furthermore, from 
other lessons we learn that God answers some prayers with a No..
Why is childlessness not considered to be the path that God has outlined for 
a couple?   God is _not_ a pro life advocate - He kind of has His own 
plans..

> Taking a life is completely different from that.  And, you'll notice that
```

- **References:** `<1e50e.2183556$Zm5.353953@news.easynews.com> <8y90e.243187$JF2.154149@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <d1s71m$30fe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d1s9d2$aoc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dccd3$424230fe$45491f85$32215@KNOLOGY.NET> <d1us9a$1o9f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <33e30$4243938f$45491f85$5288@KNOLOGY.NET> <LOP0e.1747$Pc.540@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <636da$42447f7d$45491f85$6747@KNOLOGY.NET> <m2h5e.3678979$Zm5.590479@news.easynews.com> <v8k5e.51789$Fz.42975@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <3ff9$4255f4ac$45491f85$7807@KNOLOGY.NET> <pLo5e.24361$vd.18270@tornado.tampabay.rr.com>`

```
jce wrote:
> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
> news:3ff9$4255f4ac$45491f85$7807@KNOLOGY.NET...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> societies.

Morality *can* be black and white.  And, it's not me making the 
decisions for other people - it's me, using the power I have a single 
citizen, to support those who believe the way I do about issues, whether 
life and death (such as this one), or those that aren't quite so important.

But, absolutes *have* to exist - they must!  Why is math so precise? 
Did that happen by chance?  Accidental accuracy?  To say that everything 
is relative is to turn a blind eye to nature and science.

>>>That said I often here people say "what do they do? they have no money 
>>>and they have no food and all they do is screw and have babies" about 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, and the "Just Say No" campaign has produced drug free kiddies too.

Those that *always* just said no.  Abstinence works 100% percent of the 
time it is practiced (practised, for our overseas friends).

>>Science can figure out how to create life, but that knowledge comes from 
>>something far above them.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> more than the "life" that doesn't?

No, and that is one reason I'm not in favor of certain methods of 
human-assisted conception.  Fertilizing 40 embryos, taking the 7 
"strongest" and throwing the rest away kills (for sure) 33 people. 
Chances are that all of the other 7 won't implant, but at least they 
were given a chance.

> What about the situation where the donor was not the husband or the mother 
> was borrowed?

It's still human life.

> What if the mother was a lesbian?

It's still human life.

> What about the notion that God is the creator of life and death?  Isn't that 
> sovereignty threatened by artificial means of messing with conception?

Those artificial means only exist by the permissiveness of that 
sovereign Being.  Sovereignty has no exceptions.  :)

> My understanding from the Bible is that there were many barren women and 
> each would eventually give birth after *prayer* to God.  Furthermore, from 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> plans..

I can't say what is in other people's minds.  God designed us with the 
need to reproduce - it's a biological drive, an urge that cries out to 
be fulfilled.  I don't pretend to know what God's plans are for a person 
in particular.  I *do* know the principles He has laid out and told us 
to follow in living our lives.

> "And do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing 
> of your mind, so that you may prove what the will of God is, that which is 
> good and acceptable and perfect" (Romans 12:2).

And how, in your opinion, do you "prove what the will of God is"?  There 
are Scriptures that rail against the shedding of innocent blood (the 
taking of innocent life), and there are Scriptures that exhort believers 
to share what they know with the rest of the world.  Isn't standing up 
for what you believe (from an honest reading of Scripture, not some 
pull-out-of-context mauling of it) *demanded* of believers?

>>Taking a life is completely different from that.  And, you'll notice that 
>>most anti-abortion folks are also anti-cloning - helping an egg and sperm 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Helping them get together? Sounds like a high school reunion. :-)

heh...

> Maybe I should post to alt.religion........  I'm not saying you are wrong in 
> your ideas, I'm only saying that there are other ideas.  I like to think 
> it's good to have opinions, but not always judge others by them.  Even you 
> would admit that you are not really in a position to judge the actions of 
> others - but it all makes for interesting discussion [to me at least]:-)

I don't know if you want to forage into that area - I imagine there are 
a lot of people there that have nothing better to do than take the exact 
opposite of every position posted.  :)  Kind of like here...  ;)  just 
kidding...

Opinions are different from convictions.  I am of the opinion that if 
the base lawn mowers would leave the area of my fence alone, I could do 
a lot better job of growing that grass than they have.  However, I 
wouldn't debate it (though a call to housing might be in order).  I am 
of the opinion that Pizza Hut has the best pizza.  Again, just opinion. 
  But, when you get into serious matters, life-and-death stuff, you've 
wandered into the area of convictions (or beliefs, if you will) - and 
these are the areas that I'm more likely to debate with others over.
```

```
> jce wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message

>> societies.

> Morality *can* be black and white.  And, it's not me making the decisions 
> for other people - it's me, using the power I have a single citizen, to 
> support those who believe the way I do about issues, whether life and 
> death (such as this one), or those that aren't quite so important.
Morality can be black and white if you choose them to be within your 
personal constraint system - your own ethical boundaries.
.
Given that there are entire courses of study dedicated to "moral philosophy" 
I would tend to think it's usually not black and white "across society". 
You have a right to use the means at your disposal to further your moral 
position within society as a whole. I tend to believe in the greater good - 
whereby people, in general, will make the decision that is right for them 
and society as a whole.  I believe that society intervenes when the general 
ethical boundary has been crossed.  I didn't disagree with Kevorkian's 
principles, and I didn't disagree with him being jailed, for example.

> But, absolutes *have* to exist - they must!  Why is math so precise? Did 
> that happen by chance?  Accidental accuracy?  To say that everything is 
> relative is to turn a blind eye to nature and science.
Math is often imprecise.  Think calculus, think series, think 
statistics,think quantum - want me to go on?

>>>>and they have no food and all they do is screw and have babies" about 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> time it is practiced (practised, for our overseas friends).

of failure. Therefore, as an overall method, both plans fail miserably.

snip snip  various pieces I don't feel like commenting on

>> What about the notion that God is the creator of life and death?  Isn't

>> conception?
> Those artificial means only exist by the permissiveness of that sovereign

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> fulfilled.
God provided *some* of us with a need to reproduce.

> I don't pretend to know what God's plans are for a person in particular.

> our lives.

>> "And do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the 
>> renewing of your mind, so that you may prove what the will of God is, 
>> that which is good and acceptable and perfect" (Romans 12:2).

> And how, in your opinion, do you "prove what the will of God is"?
It's not my issue to decide the will of God - never has been, never will be. 
What I think of God, and his role in both my life now and going forward is 
something only I have to reconcile.  Perhaps one needs to be careful in 
drawing the distinction between God's will, and God's principles, and the 
words of writers trying to interpret and put these principles into some 
context..

> There are Scriptures that rail against the shedding of innocent blood (the

> to share what they know with the rest of the world.  Isn't standing up for

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

I don't feel like I've taken the exact opposite of your position.

> Opinions are different from convictions.  I am of the opinion that if the 
> base lawn mowers would leave the area of my fence alone, I could do a lot 
> better job of growing that grass than they have.
The grass is always greener where the dogs are sh*tting

> However, I wouldn't debate it (though a call to housing might be in

See other thread - until they get caramelized onions they will never be the

>  But, when you get into serious matters, life-and-death stuff, you've
```

```
In article <af76e$425af5f4$45491f85$16564@KNOLOGY.NET>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>jce wrote:
>> "LX-i" <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote in message 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Morality *can* be black and white.

A torque-wrench *can* be used as a hammer... but it might not function 
very well as a torque-wrench afterwards.  Two plus two *can* be five... 
for very small values of five.  Two men *can* be providers of an 
environment which proves to be better for raising a child than a man and a 
women... and this has been demonstrated over and over again.

What '*can* be' (emphasis original) might serve to show the inventiveness 
of people in a situation... nothing more, nothing less.

[snip]

>But, absolutes *have* to exist - they must!  Why is math so precise? 
>Did that happen by chance?  Accidental accuracy?

This seems more a cry of desire than a statement of fact... it *must* be 
so!  Math is precise because the rules for that game were established as 
such... and math fails miserably in areas many folks encounter every day.  
Magnetism can be quantified, sure... and so can electricity... and I 
remember when a professor in my junior-year college physics class took 
Maxwells's equations, showed how they described physical phenomena by 
adding units to them and then manipulated them according to the rules of 
algebra many folks now take for granted...

... and my skin went cold when the speed of light (3.0 x 10(8) m/s) (where 
'10(8)' is used to signify ten-to-the-eighth-power) just... *fell* right 
out.  Math is fine for that, sure...

... but for 'beauty' or 'love' or 'honor' or 'duty' - things that some 
call the 'highest human functions', beyond the... merely mechanical and 
mathematical - it does not seem to do so well.

>To say that everything 
>is relative is to turn a blind eye to nature and science.

To say 'everything is relative' is to posit an absolute... next?

>>>>That said I often here people say "what do they do? they have no money 
>>>>and they have no food and all they do is screw and have babies" about 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Those that *always* just said no.

I always thought that the 'doing' had more influence on the results than 
the 'just saying'.

>Abstinence works 100% percent of the 
>time it is practiced (practised, for our overseas friends).

Leaving aside incidents of force... you *do* realise that you are, with 
that statement, denying the Immaculate Conception, don't you?

>
>>>Science can figure out how to create life, but that knowledge comes from 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>"strongest" and throwing the rest away kills (for sure) 33 people. 

Only if embryo=person.  Granted that every person I know of was once an 
embryo... but the existence of miscarriage demonstrates that every embryo 
will not necessarily develop into a human.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - "lie" vs "error"

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-04-12T09:23:00-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d3gsl5$2tb5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1e50e.2183556$Zm5.353953@news.easynews.com> <v8k5e.51789$Fz.42975@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <af76e$425af5f4$45491f85$16564@KNOLOGY.NET> <d3gqsr$opv$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d3gqsr$opv$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <af76e$425af5f4$45491f85$16564@KNOLOGY.NET>,

> Leaving aside incidents of force... you *do* realise that you are, with
> that statement, denying the Immaculate Conception, don't you?

Ummm ... think maybe you've mixed up some terms here ...

The doctrine of the "immaculate conception" is that Mary, as the Mother of
God, was preserved from the stain of original sin at her conception.  This
doctrine was made Roman Catholic dogma by Pope Leo IX in an "ex cathedra"
declaration on December 8, 1854AD.

What I think you're thinking of is Mary's virginity at the time of Jesus'
birth.  Roman Catholic doctrine has held that she was a lifelong virgin
since the time the contrary was defined as anathema during the Lateran
Council under Pope Martin 1 in 649AD).

Kind of like the *ascension* of Jesus into heaven, as contrasted with the
*assumption* of Mary into heaven (finally declared dogma by Pope Pius XII
"ex cathedra" on November 1, 1950).

       -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Well we had a small paperback, 'penny catechism' so it was widely 
available to us young 'uns :-). The cover was red and I'm sure you will 
recall the first question :-

Q: Who made you ?

A: God made me to know Him, to serve Him and Love Him in this world and 
in the next.

 From experience I can tell you that when folks get into priesthood, 
religious orders training etc., that 'penny catechism' becomes an 
encyclopaedeia. The above question occupies about two-thirds of a page 
and the answer, perhaps a page and a half; sometimes more. And you have 
to memorize this stuff !

 > during the reign ... er ... papacy ... of Pius XII ... ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - "lie" vs "error"

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-04-12T15:14:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d3hh7n$88s$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1e50e.2183556$Zm5.353953@news.easynews.com> <af76e$425af5f4$45491f85$16564@KNOLOGY.NET> <d3gqsr$opv$1@panix5.panix.com> <d3gsl5$2tb5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d3h7j6$57u$1@panix5.panix.com> <d3haf4$475$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <vaX6e.981973$Xk.814414@pd7tw3no>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:vaX6e.981973$Xk.814414@pd7tw3no...

> Well we had a small paperback, 'penny catechism' so it was widely
> available to us young 'uns :-). The cover was red and I'm sure you will
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> in the next.

In the US, I remember the cover being dark blue -- the "Baltimore
Catechism" -- but that was a *very* long time ago.  They may have had
different covers for the various levels, of which there wer3e four.  They
all had essentially the same questions.  Each one was geared to the audience
(pre-first-communion, pre-confirmation, post-confirmation, and teacher's
training).   And we too had to memorize the questions and the answers.  "Why
did God make me?" is question 6 in the Baltimore Catechism parts 1, 2 and 4
(the numbering scheme's entirely different in Part 3, the post-confirmation
text; there it's #150), and the answer too is slightly different:  "God made
me to know Him, to love Him, and to serve Him in this world, and to be happy
with Him for ever in Heaven" in part 1, "... happy with Him for ever in the
next" in part 2, "... happy with Him forever in the next" in parts 3 and 4
(note:  no space in "forever").

The first question in parts 1, 2 and 4 of the Baltimore is "Who made the
world?" and the answer, of course, is "God made the world."

The first question in part 3 is "What do we mean by the 'end of man'?", and
the answer is "By the 'end of man' we mean the purpose for which he was
created; namely, to know, love, and serve God."

> during the reign ... er ... papacy ... of Pius XII ... ;-)
>
> Some reign. Josef Stalin once asked, "How many divisions does the Pope
> have ?"

Well, things certainly did get a bit more relaxed under his successor, John
XXIII ... ;-)

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **Date:** 2005-04-13T17:18:22+01:00

- **References:** `<1e50e.2183556$Zm5.353953@news.easynews.com> <vaX6e.981973$Xk.814414@pd7tw3no> <d3i9q4$rhc$1@panix5.panix.com> <mgaq515s3av1kj1osml8qsamubgnf75kfq@4ax.com> <d3jft9$bjp$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <mgaq515s3av1kj1osml8qsamubgnf75kfq@4ax.com>,
>Jeff York  <ralf4@btinternet.com> wrote:

>seriousness any more.  Perhaps I should visit.

I think that "The Prisoner Appreciation Society" - or whatever they
call themselves - still have annual get-togethers there.  And of
course, thanks to Clough Williams-Elliss's covenants etc, the place
simply doesn't change.  To the extent of when they re-paint any of the
buildings, they're painted to look scruffy (actually, that's a bit
harsh, "weathered" is more like the truth).

And the hotel...  Is *superb*.  As it's the building that was used as
the "Old Folks' Home" in the series, you would feel really comfortable
there.  :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - "lie" vs "error"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca>

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <d3jivk$ah5$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
> Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Lost the thread here a bit, but are we still talking about Port Merrion 
? If so, some 35 plus years ago, down in Cornwall, tide was out about 
half a mile leaving a great swathe of rippled sand, some small pools 
with tiny shrimps etc., normally part of the seabed. Beautiful warm and 
sunny day. Couldn't take the kids into the sea, nor did we want to !

RN Fleet Air Arm helicopter above trying to shoo away a pod of some four 
or five basking sharks, not much more than 10-20 feet from the 
shoreline. Story is that species is harmless, (eats plankton) - but 
nobody was prepared to dip their toes into the sea to find out.

Jimmy
```

```
On 13-Apr-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> I prefer to take vacations closer to the equator... but you know, enough
> time has passed so that neither I nor the staff there should be taking any
> of this... stuff from a forty-year-old television program with any great
> seriousness any more.  Perhaps I should visit.

Trouble is - I'm familiar with 40 year old television programs.   For some
reason, about 30 years ago, I lost interest in TV.    I believe in Sturgeon's
Law, indicating that there is more good stuff with a larger 10% today, but I
find I value my free time differently these days.

I did watch my alma mater (MBA version) win the NCAA Men's Hockey championship
this Saturday, and I watched the end of The Masters (I play golf).   And I
expect to watch the Nuggets in the post season instead of the Avalanche in the
post season as I have in the past.    But those are local interest stories.
```

```
Different thing catch interest at different times... for some reason I 
recall a recent comic-strip that featured a couple of Olde Phartes 
reminiscing about Dayse Gone By... 'Remember when milk got delivered to 
the door in glass bottles?'  'Remeber when you'd ask *any* nearby adult to 
hold your hand as you crossed the street?'  'Remember playing ball with 
just other kids around and no adults in sight?'

'Remember what day of the week it is today?... Nope!'

>I believe in Sturgeon's
>Law, indicating that there is more good stuff with a larger 10% today, but I
>find I value my free time differently these days.

I used to believe in Sturgeon's Law... but then I figures out that 90% of 
it was crap.

>
>I did watch my alma mater (MBA version) win the NCAA Men's Hockey championship
>this Saturday, and I watched the end of The Masters (I play golf).   And I
>expect to watch the Nuggets in the post season instead of the Avalanche in the
>post season as I have in the past.    But those are local interest stories.

Come Saturday, 23 April, I will head over to my alma mater for the 
post-game party which follows the only intramural event of the year... the 
annual Croquet Match with the Naval Academy.

DD
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 12-Apr-2005, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> TV series?

It reminds me of some of the early RPG's on the Commodore Vic-20 and 64. 
  You'd ask questions or tell it what to do, and it would respond.  ("]" 
represents input, "[" represents response...)

] where am i
[ in the village
] go 10 paces forward
[ ok
] where am i
[ in the village

Not quite sure if that was the allusion the good Mr. Dwarf was making or 
not...
```

- **References:** `<1e50e.2183556$Zm5.353953@news.easynews.com> <v8k5e.51789$Fz.42975@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <af76e$425af5f4$45491f85$16564@KNOLOGY.NET> <d3gqsr$opv$1@panix5.panix.com> <d3gsl5$2tb5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 12 Apr 2005 09:23:00 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> enlightened us:

>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:d3gqsr$opv$1@panix5.panix.com...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As a lifelong Catholic, I don't recall ever learning or hearing that
Mary was a "lifelong" virgin.  She was indeed a virgin at the time of
Jeasus' conception.  But, since Jeasus had a brother, Yaï¿½aqov,
Anglicized as "James,"  I think it would be safe to assume that Mary
was not a lifelong virgin.  

There has been, as I'm sure you know, much debate about whether James
was Jeasus' brother or not.  In two gospels (Mark 6:3, Matthew
13:55-56) James is actually named as Jesusï¿½ brother, along with four
other men; at least two unnamed and unenumerated sisters are also
mentioned. 

St. Jerome (in his controversial work, Against Helvidius), argued that
although "brothers" and "sisters" are the terms used in Greek, the
reference is actually to cousins.  

Before St. Jerome, Helvidius himself had maintained during the fourth
century that the brothers and sisters were just what their name
implies -- siblings of Jesus: although he had been born of a virgin,
their father was Joseph and their mother was Mary.

In a recent work which received the Imprimatur, John P. Meier has
endorsed the Helvidian theory, to some extent on the basis of support
from second century Fathers. (see A Marginal Jew: Rethinking the
Historical Jesus I (New York: Doubleday, 1991) 332.) During that
century, a group referred to as the Ebionites even denied Jesusï¿½
virgin birth in the technical sense; his "brothers" and "sisters" were
implicitly that in the full sense of those words (see Irenaeus,
Against Heresies 1.26.1-2).

There is also a theory developed by Epiphanius during the fourth
century (Panarion 1.29.3-4; 2.66.19; 3.78.7, 9, 13), and supported by
the second-century Protoevanglium of James 9.2 and perhaps the Gospel
of Peter (according to Origenï¿½s Commentary on Matthew 10:17):11  Mary
was Jesusï¿½ mother, not Jamesï¿½, since Joseph had a wife prior to his
marriage to Mary. Josephï¿½s relatively advanced age is traditionally
held to account for his early departure from the narrative scene of
the Gospels, and that reasonable inference lends support to this
theory, while Jamesï¿½ emphasis on the Davidic identity of the Church
(see Acts 15:16) is easily accommodated on this view. Jamesï¿½ seniority
relative to Jesus might be reflected in the parable of the prodigal
(Luke 15:11-32). The story of those with Jesus seizing him in the
midst of exorcism (Mark 3:21; cf. 3:31-35) reflects the kind of almost
parental concern an older brother might feel for a younger brother.

Another, more pragmatic consideration provides support for Epiphaniusï¿½
theory, although in a modified form. As mentioned, Joseph disappears
from the scene of the Gospels from when Jesus was about twelve years
old. His death at that time has been the traditional surmise, and such
a chronology has implications for understanding Jesusï¿½ relationships
with his siblings. On the Helvidian view, Mary must have given birth
to at least seven children in twelve years (Jesus, his brothers, and
two or more sisters). Assuming that not every child she gave birth to
survived infancy, more than seven labors would be required during that
period, all this within a culture that confined women after childbirth
and prohibited intercourse with a woman with a flow of blood, and
despite the acknowledged prophylactic effect of lactation and Josephï¿½s
age.

For more on this whole debate see:
http://www.bibleinterp.com/articles/Chilton_James.htm

Also another argument can be seen at:

http://www.christianseparatist.org/briefs/sb3.20.html

>Kind of like the *ascension* of Jesus into heaven, as contrasted with the
>*assumption* of Mary into heaven (finally declared dogma by Pope Pius XII
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

someone in the Royal family knew someone in the Royal family?"
```

```
although in theory you could become the next Pope :-). Cardinal 
Ratzinger (nicknamed the Panzer Kardinal) and was head of the 
'Congregation of the Faith', whatever the correct name is, (another way 
of saying the Inquisition :-) ) - would just LUV you !

OK I'm going to give the plot away, but Dan Brown's 'da Vinci Code' - 
the premise is Jesus married Mary Magdalen - which over a period of 
2,000 years the Church has tried to keep hushed up. There's one of the 
Italian cardinals wants to publish a book to refute Brown's story. And 
it is a story, and a bloody good yarn. All the cardinal will achieve is 
doubling Brown's book sales !

As regards 'Immaculate Conception' - just another bit of hokum from a 
religious group. Where's the scientific evidence ?

Well just so happens about three months back I saw a 'Nature' TV program 
on the Pacific. Can't give you the species, but there's this fish, about 
goldfish size, that is SEXLESS. To propagate the species "it" 
self-impregnates to produce and hatch small fry. So if a teensy-weensy 
fish can do it, why not some human beings ?

I don't go near a church, but I think I can consider myself also a 
lifelong catholic. It changed for me when the austere Italian, Pius XII 
was eventually replaced by John XXIII - not necessarily his intent, but 
as a result of his papacy, so much of the questioning and current 
liberalism took place - non-celibacy, birth control, women priests, 
"banjo" masses - instead of Latin and Gregorian chant etc.

He's a long-short and probably wont get a look-in, but there's a 60-year 
old Quebec cardinal who is on the 20-odd list of possible 'Popes', who 
would like to switch back to the Latin Mass - there's my boy !

Another possible 'Papa' - perhaps not very compassionate, but when 
questioned about condoms as being the means of controlling the killer 
AIDS in Africa, the Nigerian cardinal responded, "Nobody died from 
chastity".

Jimmy
```

```
http://www.informationweek.com/showArticle.jhtml?articleID=160503515

IT Confidential by John Soat

Includes:

... BECAUSE PEOPLE WILL BET ON ANYTHING. Speaking of online gambling, a
colleague at our sister online publication, TechWeb, contacted gambling sites to
find out the betting line on the new Pope. ("Online Gaming Sites May Have Inside
Track On Next Pope"). Odds-on favorite to win when the College of Cardinals
meets to pick a new leader for the Roman Catholic Church is Italy's Cardinal
Dionigi Tettamanzi, at anywhere from odds of 9-2 to 11-4. Antonia Sharpe, a
spokeswoman for Betfair.com, notes that Pope wagering on its site has been under
way for 18 months. Betting was slow initially, but has picked up sharply, with
some $70,000 wagered so far. The other Cardinals, in order of betting odds on
Betfair: Francis Arinze of Nigeria, at 15-to-2; O. Rodiguez Maradiaga of
Honduras, 8-to-1; Claudio Hummes of Brazil, also at 8-to-1; and J. Ratizinger of
Germany, 29-to-1.
```

```
OK so latter is translation from 'Sancte Spiritus' but in 'Anglo' I
still prefer Holy Ghost - no doubt from German Heilliger Geist. (If the
last bit is wrong - blame Babelfish !)

I had a time-out on my Server so couldn't post this earlier. So I see 
Chuck is favouring Ratzinger and Arinze. Like JPII they are both 'worldly'.

Prior to priesthood JPII was into amateur dramatics, quite a nice 
looking young man and always played the leading man opposite a rather 
attractive Polish girl. A very good two-hour retrospective on him by CBC 
illustrated this. According to his Jewish childhood friend they were 
'close'. Then it broke up; discretely none of their group asked why. Nor 
did the 'girl' offer any information, although on a subsequent visit to 
the Vatican the TV showed them hugging - not just your usual peck on the 
cheek. He also did time breaking up stones in a quarry - then became a 
seminarian.

Ratzinger - the Panzer Kardinal - that I think refers to the fact that 
he did his conscription service in the post-war Wermacht. Appears he was 
very close to JPII.

Arinze - son of a wealthy Nigerian business man. He converted to 
catholicism.

Just got to wait for the white smoke guys - plus, for first time, the 
peals of a joyful bell !

Jimmy
```

```
On Thu, 14 Apr 2005 17:43:08 GMT, "James J. Gavan"
<jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> enlightened us:

>Howard Brazee wrote:
>> On 14-Apr-2005, "James J. Gavan" <jgavandeletethis@shaw.ca> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy

The pope doesn't necessarily come from the ranks of the cardinals
although that is the more common route.  There are black bishops from
the United States and Africa.  One from Nigeria has been mentioned as
a possible papal candidate.

A North American pope wouldn't have to be a US citizen.  Could be
Canadian or Mexican.   Given the huge number of Catholics that are
Hispanic, a Mexican pope is not out of the realm of possibility
either.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

someone in the Royal family knew someone in the Royal family?"   
--Robin Williams

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - "lie" vs "error"

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2005-04-13T12:12:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d3jqug$1lui$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<1e50e.2183556$Zm5.353953@news.easynews.com> <v8k5e.51789$Fz.42975@tornado.tampabay.rr.com> <af76e$425af5f4$45491f85$16564@KNOLOGY.NET> <d3gqsr$opv$1@panix5.panix.com> <d3gsl5$2tb5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <m0jq51pnrr7ntef5el54egfe6nkso1knsr@4ax.com>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.rr.NOSPAM.com> wrote in message
news:m0jq51pnrr7ntef5el54egfe6nkso1knsr@4ax.com...

> As a lifelong Catholic, I don't recall ever learning or hearing that
> Mary was a "lifelong" virgin.  She was indeed a virgin at the time of
> Jeasus' conception.  But, since Jeasus had a brother, Ya'aqov,
> Anglicized as "James,"  I think it would be safe to assume that Mary
> was not a lifelong virgin.

Well, I don't dispute that there may have been a shift in Roman Catholic
thought on this matter since the days of Vatican II.

But to say that as a lifelong Catholic you never heard or learned that Mary
was a "lifelong" virgin strikes me as strange, given the wording in the
Confiteor in the ordinary of the Roman Mass, which, so far as I know, still
includes the words "I confess to Almighty God, ... to blessed Mary ever
Virgin ... Therefore I beseech blessed Mary ever Virgin ..." (or, if you
prefer the Latin version, "Confiteor Deo omnipotenti ... beatae Mariae
semper Virgini ... Ideo precor beatam Mariam semper Virginem ...").  Has the
wording of the Mass been changed recently in this area?

Whatever Msgr. Meier might argue as a possibility, I'd have to suggest that
the traditional view of the Church is represented by Tertullian, Jerome,
Augustine and several sainted Popes on the subject, and I'd be really
surprised if this view had changed in recent years without making big news
somewhere!

Out of curiosity, in Meier's "A Marginal Jew", who signed the Imprimatur?
Did it also receive a Nihil Obstat, and if so, from whom?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> enlightened us:

>

As I noted in my reply to James, I just don't recall learning that
concept in catechism.  That's not to say they didn't teach it. And for
whatever reason, I just never put the words you quoted and the concept
of being a lifelong virigin together.  Now that you have stated it, I
see where "ever Virgin" implies lifelong.  

>Whatever Msgr. Meier might argue as a possibility, I'd have to suggest that
>the traditional view of the Church is represented by Tertullian, Jerome,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Meier is a Roman Catholic Bible scholar and often signs the Imprimatur
himself.  In the case of this book, the Imprimatur was signed in New
York on June 25, 1991 by The Most Rev. Patrick J. Sheridan, V.G.

It did not receive a Nihil Obstat.

Regards,
```

```
Aye, there's the rub.  Although the Bishop found nothing objectionable
(Imprimatur) the diocese's censor either didn't agree there was nothing
doctrinally wrong with it or wasn't offered the opportunity to examine it.
Where was Sheridan assigned?

Apparently Imprimatur doesn't mean what it used to.  See
http://www.kensmen.com/catholic/imprimatur.html .
```
